DeepMind-- the Podcast." I'm Professor Hannah Fry. Right, agents, they are
here, or almost here, and they are probably
all anyone is going to be talking about in 2025. But they are definitely not new. My guest on today's
episode is someone who last came on
the podcast in 2019 to talk to me about the
multi-agent system he was working on. It could beat the
professional StarCraft players at their own game,
and eventually went on to achieve Grandmaster status. But how have agents
evolved since then? What can they do now? How have the advances
in language models and multimodal AI change things? And how do you possibly go
about building something that can make autonomous
decisions on behalf of its user? Now, I should tell you, if
you want a primer on agents, you can watch our episode
with Frederic Besse that we recorded
over the summer. But for now, Oriol
Vinyals is Vice President of Drastic Research and
co-tech-lead of Gemini. And it's fair to say we've got
quite a lot to catch up on. Oriol, welcome back
to the podcast. ORIOL VINYALS: Hi,
thank you for having me. HANNAH FRY: What is
Drastic Research? ORIOL VINYALS:
Well, I keep telling my team they have
to think drastic, meaning don't just do
the incremental stuff that everyone is thinking about. Try to drastically think what
will happen in a few years time, and then try to
backport those ideas and then execute today
with that mindset in mind. So that's what drastic means. But yeah, it's a word
that I use a lot. HANNAH FRY: I think when
I last got to see you, you had been working
on an agent that could use a keyboard and a
mouse to do things draw pictures in Paint or play StarCraft. And, well, things have moved
on quite a bit since then. ORIOL VINYALS: So those
agents, at the time, you took sort of a very
generic set of principles, very simple principles in the
field of machine learning, and you would basically
specialize a model on one task. And what we were
doing at the time is have a curriculum of tasks
that were more and more and more difficult, right? So when we last spoke, for
instance, in video games, we were looking at
StarCraft, which is one of the most complex
modern strategy games out there. And of course,
DeepMind is notorious for having started the
trend with Atari, which is a fairly simple game of
left, right, hit the paddle and hit the ball and off you go. So that is what the
algorithms themselves, we tried to push for them to be
very general so we can keep climbing this ladder of
difficulty, curriculum of games, and doing more and
more complex things. And right now what
has happened is even the models we train are broadly
applicable to many more things than the models we
developed back then were. So think about the process of
creating this digital brain hasn't changed that much. But what that brain
was able to do was reasonably narrow,
although very complex, like playing StarCraft
or playing Go. Right now, these models can
do quite a lot more broad applications and, of course,
talking to us, chatbots, et cetera, et cetera. HANNAH FRY: So back then,
reinforcement learning was your main lever, I guess. How different are things now? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so
algorithmically, actually, the process of AlphaGo and
actually AlphaStar, those two had the same set of sequence
of algorithms applied to creating this digital brain. And it's not actually
that different from how current large language
models or multimodal models are created today. There's two basic
steps that have been pretty constant
throughout many years in many of the projects
that we've worked on, which we can call the first
one pretraining or imitation learning. That is, you start
with random weights, you have an algorithm that will
try to imitate lots of data that humans have created
to either play a game or, in this case, all of the
internet, all of the knowledge available to us. And in that first
stage, you just adapt the weights to try to
imitate that data as well as possible. HANNAH FRY: And these
weights are, essentially inside each of the neurons
is a series of numbers that kind of describes how it's
connected to everything else? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
so basically there are units of computations
that are neurons. And the connections
between neurons are what actually
you have as weights. So you can imagine
that there's a neuron, there's a few neurons
connected to it. And you're basically
adding all the activations from the incoming neurons there
multiplied by the weights. And those weights are the
only things that move. And the inputs
excite the neurons. It's pretty much how a brain
works with some freedom of, yeah, creativity. HANNAH FRY: OK, if we
were to do an analogy, it's almost like you've got
the neurons and you're like, water is flowing through
it and the weights is like the width of the
pipes between the neurons? ORIOL VINYALS:
Yeah, that's right. And then you can imagine having
millions of neurons and billions or even trillions of pipes. And that is what we spend
most to compute actually training these models,
especially language models, is in this pretraining
or imitating all the data that we have available to us. HANNAH FRY: OK, so you've
now got this gigantic network with loads of pipes going
between all the neurons. And that's your
imitation phase done. Next bit, if you were doing,
say, AlphaGo or AlphaZero, you would then get
it to play itself. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
so this model now is reasonably good at playing
moves that look human-like. So that means, of
course, the sentences are very plausible
sentences in English. Or if it was playing a game, it
would click things reasonably to move pieces on the
board and whatnot. But what this model
hasn't done is learn that these
actions yield reward. That's the bit of reinforcement
learning or post-training, which is the second
phase of training. So you can write a
poem by just, hey, just how does a poem on the
internet look like on average? But then the question is, well,
I want only the good poems. So how can I further adjust
these pipes based on a signal that now, having written
now a whole poem, would give a score
of 0 or 1, let's say. And if it's a mediocre
poem, you get a 0. If it's a good
poem, you get a 1. Again, for a game
analogy, which is what we use reinforcement
learning traditionally, if you win at the game, you get a 1. If you lose, you get a 0. And then you further
adjust the weights. But now, instead of
imitating humans, you're just saying
forget, I want to go beyond what
humans could do and try to really get all my
poems to be the perfect poem or all my chess games
to be the perfect game. And in language models,
this second phase, which is reinforcement
learning post-training, tends to be fairly short-lived
because we do not have access to super clean reward
as you've won the game or you lost the
game, when you do self-play in traditional
board games, for example. HANNAH FRY: So once
that's done, that's all the stuff that goes
on behind the scenes. And then you're like,
hold it right there. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: Stay exactly
where you are, everybody. We're going to take just
basically a snapshot of this entire network, and
that is what you actually get to access as a user. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so now
this amazing process finished. These weights are
super precious, right? So this configuration
you found, you've really spent months to finesse
it, to tweak everything. And now you will
never move it anymore. So training is over. You're not changing the
configuration anymore. You might want to make
it super efficient. So say you find that, oh, look,
this neuron is not that useful. It's not used for anything. You remove it, so everything
becomes faster and cheaper to run it at scale. And then as a user, you
just get the same weights. Everyone gets the same
weights we've trained. That's what we call
Gemini 1.5 Flash. That just means a
set of weights that are frozen, will
not change, will not further train or anything. So those two steps
actually pretty much are identical from
AlphaGo to AlphaStar to current large
language models. And of course, there's
details that matter. And the field has
evolved, certainly, but the principle is pretty
much unchanged, actually. HANNAH FRY: Because under
the hood, as it were, there are differences between-- I don't know, I'm
thinking like DQN here, which was the Atari example,
or the types of algorithms that are used in AlphaGo or then
again, in the large language models, the architecture
is different, right? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so
there's a few components that go into then what
the digital brain is. One is the architecture. So there are these
neural networks. Now we have the transformers,
which we certainly didn't have back in the DQN days. So there's always some
sort of breakthroughs in architectures that are better
at learning from the data. But then from
transformers to today, it's almost all
about little tweaks. Even if you look
at AlphaFold, which also is fueled by a transformer,
what the teams do for years sometimes is just to
find little tweaks on, hey, let's remove
this set of neurons, let's add another layer,
let's make this a bit wider. So the brain shape
changes a little bit, and that makes it or
breaks it sometimes, in terms of the
performance achieved. HANNAH FRY: So if these
are all the things that have been achieved so far,
the goal, as I understand it, is to create more
agentic behavior, to get these things to
make autonomous decisions. How did these help
to achieve that end? ORIOL VINYALS:
Yeah, so let's zoom in a little bit on
the current trend. We call it large language
models, but they're multimodal. I think we had an episode
earlier covering heavily the multimodality
aspect, how good it is to be able
to add an image, then ask something, a
follow-up question, and so on. So this score, we
will still improve it, this set of weights that
do these amazing inferences about the input. What's this image about? What's the user asking? Can I write a better poem? Can I just make it
longer or whatever? Like all these interactions we
all get to play with these days. But this is just a component. And we can think, hey,
this is now our CPU. And we can add more
to it, around it. What if the model could go
off and do research for you, for example? One example, we were
already thinking about that back in the day, I
could ask a model, a language model or a visual
language model to learn to play the game of StarCraft. That's a very different approach
to say, create one agent that does play the game. In this other example, it
could go online, watch videos about the game. It could, of course,
download the game to start interacting with it
to learn, oh, yeah, I know, I get it. Do research online, go to
forums, read the forums. Go play and figure out that
it's weak at this thing and improve and so on. And after, literally,
it could be weeks, it sends you an email that says,
I now know how to play the game. Let's play. That's not a reality
that's that far away. But these models all of a
sudden actually do something, take some actions,
and learn anything new that is available to them. And that's pretty
powerful to think about. It's what pushes the
generality the most. And that's what makes the
AGI, as many people call it, feel closer. HANNAH FRY: So if I
understand it correctly then, it's almost like
the stuff that we have at the moment, the
large language models, the multimodal models,
whatever you want to call them, that's the central core. But the next step is
that you build stuff on top of that central
core, that it can go off and take off the stabilizers
and go off and do its own thing. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, exactly. If it has access to
all the knowledge and it can use its time to

quite drastically. Although of course,
we're not going to need that for everything. I mean, if we ask a question
like, hey, I like rice. What should I prepare tonight? Probably no need to do a very
deep dive into thinking or just going off for three weeks. Then you'll probably not be very
happy about the waiting time, right? But I think to
push the frontier, you're giving a digital body
to the computer so it can not only just think and give an
instruction or a word output, but it can also go off
and do things online or on documents that you
might upload or whatever. And ask very, very complex
questions and personalized to you, et cetera, et cetera. HANNAH FRY: I like that idea,
this central core and then you're giving it a digital body. You've got the electric
brain, and now you're giving it a digital body. ORIOL VINYALS: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: OK, so in
terms of the electric brain and in terms of this
core, this processor, let me just ask you
a bit about that. I guess we should be thinking
about Gemini here, right? ORIOL VINYALS: Mm-hmm. HANNAH FRY: Which is essentially
what we're talking about, the multimodal model
that you guys have. I know that one of the
big ideas for large models was just to scale it up, to
get them bigger and bigger and bigger and
bigger and bigger. Do you think that the results
that we've seen from scaling have plateaued by now? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, it's
a very important question. We have studied how as you
make the models larger, that is how many neurons
literally these models have, how do they become
better at certain tasks that we have clear metrics
from the whole machine learning community? For example, one that is
very simple to understand is machine translation. So how good the models
are at translating between two languages
as you scale, as you go from
millions to billions to potentially
trillions of neurons, you can see the
performance keep improving. Now, even when you
do those studies, one trick is that
it looks linear, but you have to plot
logarithmic axis. What that means in lay
terms is that, let's say from the last three years,
we had some improvement, you shouldn't expect
the same improvement in the next three years. It's actually exponentially
hard to get there. So that means the
compute investment, which of course, also advances
at the super linear rate, but perhaps not as good as
these sort of trends suggest, you would just see some
diminishing returns. Because simply
scaling the x-axis, the number of parameters, you
need to go 10x to see the same improvement. And that just creates
some pressure to, hey, maybe we
can't scale as much and we need to think
about other ways to scale to make
the models better. HANNAH FRY: The example
I give to my students is like, if you've got a
room that's really messy, the first 10 minutes
that you spend tidying, it's going to make a
massive difference. You pick up all
the dirty plates, put away all the
dirty washing, fine. But once you're like 7 hours
in, that extra 10 minutes, it's not going to make
any difference at all. And that's essentially
where we are, right? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, that's
exactly a very good analogy. And in fact, that
analogy can even apply to then the
performance of the models. Even if you have extremely
good performance, if you want these models
to be 100% factual, they will never
make something up-- we know that if you probe them,
you can make them say some things that are not real-- even that last mile
also is super hard, which creates some interesting
challenges to deploy them at scale. HANNAH FRY: So OK,
I hear what you're saying about how there's
diminishing returns in all of this. But in terms of how you
make these things better, how you make these
models better, is it just data,
computational power, and size? Are those the only things, the
levers that you have to pull? ORIOL VINYALS:
Yeah, so certainly, if you froze the
architecture, let's say for the next year,
no innovation, we just scale because there's
better hardware coming out-- HANNAH FRY: Just
make it bigger, yeah. ORIOL VINYALS: --make it
bigger, that certainly would have a trend
that would look OK. But what's happened,
and certainly in Gemini, we have other innovations,
other tricks, techniques, details from how to order
the data that you present the model with, to the
details of the architecture, to how to run the training
process, how long to run it for. What kind of data do we
actually present the model? How do we filter? Do we present more data that's
high quality, less data that's low quality? All sorts of different what
we call hyperparameters and, of course other
algorithmic advances, we also investigate
fairly carefully. Because the process of
training a model is expensive. So we need to be extremely
careful with piling up innovation so that
eventually when we are ready, we have enough innovation. And also probably we'll
have a better scale to run for the next
iteration of models. We run it, and then we
get algorithmic, not only breakthroughs
through data and compute. HANNAH FRY: I guess the other
thing about this scaling stuff is that there's no limit,
really, to the number of nodes that you can put in. Maybe there's no
limit, in theory, to the computational
power that you put in. But there is a limit to the
data that you can put in. There's a limit to the number of
human words that are out there. ORIOL VINYALS: Good point. So I think there is
a limit on the nodes. Because how you scale

single chip, hardware chip. So now you have a mesh of
chips that are communicating. There's certain limits
like speed of light, et cetera, et cetera. HANNAH FRY: [LAUGHS] ORIOL VINYALS: So
there starts to be a time where the efficiency
of training such a big model is also just not worth it,
even from a utilization of the hardware at your
disposal, but very good point. The other bit that is critical
on this pretraining, imitate all the data, is that
we do not have what we call infinite data regime. There's finite data. And so as soon as
the models need to-- you can think, well, let's
train on all the data. If you want to train-- HANNAH FRY: Everything
humans have ever read-- ORIOL VINYALS: Everything. HANNAH FRY: --or written. ORIOL VINYALS: All
of the internet. So we're just starting to think,
OK, we're running out of data. There are techniques

English, I mean, 60%. I don't know what's

rewrite the same knowledge in different ways. We're exploring those. That's kind of a research
area that many people are starting to invest. Because if you run out
of data, the scaling laws punish you even more. HANNAH FRY: So for
example, then, you could get Gemini to write its
own version of the internet, and then use that to train
a new version of Gemini? ORIOL VINYALS: Yes. HANNAH FRY: Is there
a danger, though, that if you start feeding in
the output of the same model, that you can end up creating
these little, well, unhelpful feedback loops? ORIOL VINYALS:
They certainly can do some interesting
experiments to test ideas like this one you
just mentioned. And indeed, that is, on the
surface, not a good idea. The model suffers if you
just ask it to recreate all of the internet. And indeed, a priori
from information an content point of

it has, how could you create new information? I don't know. These ideas might
help a little bit because there's machine
learning deficiencies that we're not at that
fundamental ability to extract all the information
truly from the internet. We have good algorithms,

we'll see, yeah. HANNAH FRY: I guess I just want

--information directly? Probably not. HANNAH FRY: Oh, I

imitation phase, which is what we've been

and AlphaZero and many more got better by
playing themselves. Does that apply here as well? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, that's one
of the main open challenges-- scaling, not only pretraining
but postraining or reinforcement learning. So the beauty about
reinforcement learning in games is that there is a set
of rules that are coded. And if you've won,
you know you've won. There is kind of a program
that if you play chess and you've won, it
will check everything. OK, that's a check mate. Congratulations,
you've won the game. HANNAH FRY: A clear
metric of success. ORIOL VINYALS: A clear metric. Now, in language, much trickier. Is this a better poem than this? Good luck discussing
this, even amongst us. So the generality again makes
computing exactness very hard. Is this a better
summary of the movie? Is this the most interesting
bit of this video? It's very hard to quantify. But we can try, and we do try. You train a model, and based
on some human preferences, roughly you just say, OK,
try to now generalize. So if I ask a model to
criticize its own output, it's not going to do that bad. It's going to be good maybe
80% of the time, which is not terrible. But it can give you some signal. But at that point, you
start saying, well, now you climb this metric. You have this imperfect
way to assess performance. But now we're going
to start training against this reward
that is not perfect. What the model is
going to do is exploit the weaknesses of the reward. Maybe using the chess example,
imagine that I had a bug, and if a pawn is in a certain
position, you always win. And it's a position that
no one would play ever. So maybe no one
knows this exists. But now you ask an
algorithm to please explore everything
and try to discover how to win at this game. All of a sudden, you're
going to find, hey, oh, if I move the first
pawn to this position, no one plays this
opening, You've. Won the game. Certainly, you the algorithm
has nailed the game. And then a researcher goes
and sees how you play chess, and it's just terrible. HANNAH FRY: Naughty
AI, basically that's what we're talking about. ORIOL VINYALS: So
that's the challenge. Basically you're
finding exploits rather than really learning what
a good poem means truthfully. HANNAH FRY: Can you not
just add in another player? So add in another model,
which is the ultimate arbiter? ORIOL VINYALS: Good suggestion,
but then the problem is, how do you train that model? We have only a finite
notion of what's a good poem by some experts
that we might ask, hey, compare these two poems and so on. So there's just a
limited amount of data we have to train these arbiters. So the ground truth
might be to ask someone that is the expert, of course. And if we could, we would. But that's not scalable. Then imagine how slow
it would be to say, OK, I have a parameter update
that in three seconds, I found. Now please review these
10,000 things by an expert. Because that's the
source of truth. And we don't have enough data
to train a good enough reward model. So again, there are some ideas. But intuitively, you
clearly understand, but the problem is we don't
have access to the ground truth. HANNAH FRY: That's it. It's like you're feeling around
in the dark with oven gloves on. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: [LAUGHS] ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: [LAUGHS] You're not
even completely sure that there are solid objects-- ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: --to grab onto. OK, so if that's the core then,
that's the electronic brain, and now we're building the

quite a lot of work on that too, hasn't there? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so when
you start thinking, well, what are the main

limited access to so they can see beyond what's in their
weights, which are frozen, to be able to gather knowledge

very well at Google. Another one is to
give them the ability to run the code they write. And then, of course, maybe
even more broadly, that could be more general is
giving them the ability to maybe interact with a browser
that has access to the internet. With all of these,

that just means protect these environments so that the
models, even if they're not that advanced, wouldn't do
something that is unintended. So there's the whole
safety aspect of this that as you move
beyond the model, it starts to be
quite interesting. But if we're just dreaming
what would be possible, by having these tools available
to the models, all of a sudden they can start doing
much more advanced things beyond what was the training
corpus that we used at the time. They can rely on the
latest news to explain us or to summarize what
was the main thing yesterday that happened. All these kind of things, you
need to give them these tools. HANNAH FRY: OK, so how does
reasoning fit into all of this then? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, reasoning
is interesting, right? So what I described
could be summarized as, hey, I want to know
what happened yesterday, then I could just say,
look, maybe personalize it a little bit. So I could describe in words,
I could say, hey hey, model, I'm Oriol, I'm interested
in this and that. My political views
are this or that. Give me a positive take
on the news yesterday. And the model would probably
search, retrieve all the news, and then, given what
I asked it to do, just do it in a way that I
like and I find it enjoyable. Maybe if I don't like
it, I can even go and then say, I didn't like
this or this is not a good joke. And then we could iterate a
little bit in a conversation. Now, reasoning is a bit of
a different axis of scaling. And so you could
imagine the model deciding what kind
of intermediate steps to do to give me
a better answer. So imagine there's
100 news outlets that Google search retrieves. Maybe the model
decides, hey, I'm just not going to
read this and just try to summarize
it all at once, I'm going to summarize each
of the 100 articles first. So that means the
model decided I'm going to write a summary
for each of the 100 pages. I'm going to write it not
to the user, to myself. And now it has
hundreds summaries. And maybe the next
step it decides to do is, I'm going to
group these by topics. Then it decides one of the
articles looks suspicious, so maybe it goes online
and checks if in any forums someone discusses
like, oh, this might be not truthful because of the
author and so on and so forth. So it can do a lot of
steps to do research. And it could do this
for quite a while. And only when the
model says, well, I think now I have a much
better quality answer, then it will give you
the few-word summary. But now it had all this time
to do much more processing on the information that
was available to it. And that inference
time compute, we hope that the more time
we give to the model, the better it's
going to summarize the news, the better it's
going to write a poem, the better it's going
to, of course, do math. But that's certainly
another axis of scaling which we're starting to quite
unlock, we hope to unlock. And again, we'll break a bit of
the scaling laws and the limits that we see in pure pretraining. HANNAH FRY: Does this
also include planning? Could it look at your calendar,
work out when your payday was, maybe know that the January
sales are coming up soon, and tell you to postpone booking
your holiday for a few days? ORIOL VINYALS: That
can get very complex, but of course, when you factor
in things like personalization and when to do things because
of all the other things that are ongoing, you have
more sources of information. You need to collect them and
then give the best answer. And it stops being what
color is the sky, which is not that simple to answer. I was thinking
about that example. We had a very early paper
where we have that example, as, oh, something that
language models can do. Amazing, right? You don't program them to
answer, but they answer. But then, actually
even the answer is quite nuanced if you
start thinking, oh, yeah, planets, and what
time of the day? Is it cloudy or not? So the thinking
and the planning, yeah, that's something
that these models can do. HANNAH FRY: I'm reminded, I
had a conversation with Dennis probably back in 2019. And he was talking about
the Kahneman and Tversky ideas of how the human brain has
almost two systems of thinking, the sort of quick,
instinctive, intuition based, and then the much slower,
calculated, the way that you do maths and chess. And Demis was saying
that that second one has been traditionally easier
for us to do with computers. But now we're seeing the much
quicker instinctive stuff. But you're sort of talking about
putting the two together, right? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, right. Probably what Demis
was talking about is systems too, which is indeed
one that you reflect a bit more. And in games it's very clear. You just could say, oh, this
move feels right, you just move. But if you think and ponder,
you might get to a better move. The challenge is
that, now because we are in such general
direction-- these models can do anything, anything, literally. You just do whatever
you want, upload an image, talk about the news. So what it means
to have this deeper thinking is so
domain specific that, how are you going to do that? And there's a few answers. But the one I like is
like, well, these models are very general. To add the ability
to think on top of a very general
set of capabilities, you probably need a
general way to think. And so you use the model
itself to generate how it should think about anything. And the model will
come up with, oh, I'm going to summarize each article. I'm going to do this
and that and that. And it is not us programming it. That's a very deep insight. Now is it the only way to do it
and is it the best way to do it? Early days. HANNAH FRY: Yeah. ORIOL VINYALS: Five years. We'll see. HANNAH FRY: [LAUGHS] Exactly,
I'll talk to you in 2029. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: OK, I'm
thinking now though also about lots of the things
that felt very important back, five years ago. And a lot of it was about
inspiration from neuroscience. So I suppose in a
way here, you're talking about planning
and reasoning. But memory was the
other really big one. And has that come through? People talk about long context
and short context a lot. I suppose that is working
memory in a way, isn't it? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
there's techniques that are out there that you
can apply to a language model. There are at the
very least three. And they're reasonably
simple to explain. The first way in which
we have a system that memorizes all of the internet
is by literally doing the pretraining step. That's literally a
memorization step in a particular format, which
is we have these weights, they're random, and
then we assemble them in these amazing architectures. Now, the second level is
maybe I explained a little bit how you would give the tool of
a search engine such as Google to the model. That, you could claim,
is what neuroscientists would call episodic
memory, which, as a human, maybe it's like we have these
memories from a long time ago. They're not very precise. So they tend to be
a bit more fuzzy. If I have to think, oh, what
was my first day at Google? I remember bits and pieces,
being in a room or someone I met or whatnot. HANNAH FRY: The gist. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
the gist, right? Now, interestingly,
these models maybe don't have that limitation. You can literally get an article
written many years ago online, and it's going to have
all the images, everything will be perfect,
reconstructed perfectly. So that second mode called
episodic memory, clearly we're seeing that when you
integrate especially powerful search engines into our models. And then the third
one is what you could call working memory, which
actually the whole of thinking that I described is one of. If we take every news
article, but then we want to create summaries, find
how they relate to each other, criticize some of
them, this starts to combine working
memory, meaning I'm going to have a scratchpad
of the summaries, the issues that I think I'm finding. And that, when we call
short or long context, generally, we mean this last
bit, the working memory, whether you have a thousand
tokens, which means I couldn't possibly do much. I can retrieve articles. It's already over
thousand words. Not much I can do
to summarize them. Or it can be massive,
in which case you have many more possibilities
to do reasoning on top of that, and so on and so forth. And so one of the
breakthroughs of the year, actually, we're still in 2024. HANNAH FRY: Just. ORIOL VINYALS: Yes,
but it was just to enable millions
of tokens in context, which enables many things. You can retrieve
something from the past, but then bring it
forward and then do a very detailed analysis. That's a bit of the examples
of, we have a movie, we can upload a movie
or some very long video and start doing summarization. The fact we upload it
is more episodic memory, but then now we
have it in memory. It all fits in memory. We can do quite a lot of
associations within each frame, each object in the movie,
and so on and so forth. HANNAH FRY: Is a longer
context window better always? Because I'm just
thinking about, I don't know how much you guys
are still using neuroscience as an inspiration for
what you're doing. But the human memory, there's
a limit to the working memory. There's certainly some
times where you're like, my brain is full and I'm done. ORIOL VINYALS: Sometimes
brain is an inspiration, but computers certainly
have advantages. We should build on
its strengths, right? So perhaps the fact that they
can have literally in memory every Wikipedia
article, whatever it is, we can't, but if the model
can, well, there you go. You have new capabilities. But also, it might be
too confusing to have too much information even
for these neural networks. So it might be a good
idea to compress. So that's where
you probably want to push for getting some
inspiration for how we might do what we do, which
is quite amazing, in terms of memory
retrieval and so on. HANNAH FRY: Yeah. This is why you're leading
up Drastic Research. ORIOL VINYALS: Yes. What we want to
do with the models should be definitely
inspiring and forward looking. And then what are the main
limits of the technology? And then try to, of
course, place the bets and inspire the teams
to finding solutions around the critical components. HANNAH FRY: But some of the
bets that you've already made have come off. I know there's been
a big announcement of a dizzying number
of new features that have just come out. Can we talk through
some of them? But then maybe also, talk to
me about the different skills that we've already
spoken about and how they appear in each of these. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so we
have quite a few systems around our best Gemini models. So one of the things that
we've done is update to 2.0. We're seeing a
generational leap. Even if you say, look,
let's not scale anymore, can we get better quality? So we've done it sort of again. These models are
faster, they're cheaper, and they're actually better. HANNAH FRY: Basically,
Gemini has got better. That's it. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, Gemini
has got better, but not only because we scaled. HANNAH FRY: Sure. ORIOL VINYALS: That's
one of the main messages. HANNAH FRY: Tell me more
about the agentic capabilities that you've brought to Gemini. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, so we're
releasing a companion in Chrome where you can just
type to do a task that maybe some of
these tasks are tricky. Because I partly enjoy them, but
also I partly don't like them. So I'm thinking now very
clearly about trips. So you travel and you look for
hotels or flights or whatnot, and a lot of it feels like, oh,
I wish this could be automated. But at the same
time, I wouldn't just want to not be part
of this journey. So I guess the kind of
thing we're releasing is something that hopefully
will automate parts of the more trivial steps or
repeated or things that need automation
because I can't be bothered to click everything. So we're adding an agent that
you can ask it to do something for you, and then it's going
to, again, through thinking and through acting on the
basic clicking on links and so on, try to
solve the task for you. And that's quite an exciting,
both research challenge, opportunity. Because it's a very general
environment for a very general agent and model, ultimately. And some examples that we've had
again in the early prototypes, we can ask to play a game,
which of course, goes back to the roots of
DeepMind, on the browser. And it did OK. It finds a website, it
starts playing the game. It's kind of a cool connection
to the more general you are, then the more you can
treat environments where you had specialization
in the past, as now, oh, I just can
type it and it just goes and learn to
play this game. We're not quite
there, but this is a glimpse of maybe
where we could go with this kind of technology. HANNAH FRY: You're right
that it does really bring us back to that
thing that you were doing so many years ago, which
was something that could use a keyboard and a mouse. It's a really similar thing. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, even
the actions are very similar. They understand the
screen and, given what you ask, where would
you click and so on, that's the same sort of actions
even that very general games you would have to interact with. The difference is the
goal there is narrow. It's just one game and
the same kind of screens. Whereas here, it's the whole
web, which is pretty vast. HANNAH FRY: Well, OK. But then so I'm sort of
imagining what you could do now. Could it look in your calendar? Could you say, I want to
go on holiday next year, and it could look
in your calendar and work out when
the best week was, know your budget, et cetera,
et cetera, et cetera? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
so these models are not far from being
able to automate this. So now it's a matter of making
it better, making it safe. There's a lot of steps. But if you just fast
forward, anything a human can do on a
browser, these things can do in principle. And then if you make
them really understand what you want and really
good through thinking and other techniques, they'll
get better and better. And they'll be probably
faster and maybe in some cases much better than
you at doing those. So that's the dream. And these are
super early stages, but it's also super exciting. And I think certainly
next year, we're going to see a lot
of experimentation around this idea of intersecting
language models agentically with browser or
computers more generally. HANNAH FRY: How about coding? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, coding
is a great one as well. We are also releasing tools
for software engineering, which of course, they generally
require not only, hey, here is a perfect description
of a puzzle about coding and please write me the code. And by the way, I
know how to test it. It's more iterative. You have to write code and run
the code and so on and so forth. So we are putting that
capability, as well, forward from an agentic point of view. Games are very important. And of course, that
was a means to an end to develop powerful algorithms. But it's also very
interesting to think about how these very
powerful multimodal models start to understand games
and can aid users to entertain doing a game session,
give them advice, or tell a joke about the
game, or whatnot. So we're also experimenting with
this sort of game companion. HANNAH FRY: OK,
all of these things that you're talking
about, this is sounding very close
to intelligence that is quite general. Are we getting close to AGI? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
this is a good question. Look, I was thinking about
this earlier this week. If 10 years ago, 5
years ago even, I would have been given
the models today, and I would say, look, there's
a secret lab, this is a model, play with it and tell me if
you think this is actually close to a general intelligence;
I would have claimed, oh, yeah, that comes from a
future where AGI basically either has happened or I can see
that this is very close to it. So the closer you are,
the more you find, oh, but it hallucinates. Of course, that's
very important. But I think, just
zooming out, it just feels like, OK, it's
getting pretty close. HANNAH FRY: But then
DeepMind's mission statement, solve intelligence, that
sort of intelligence, super intelligence,
something that surpasses human intelligence,
do you think that scaling is enough to get us there? Or do you think that
we need something else? ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah,
Google DeepMind has this mission to obviously
intersect intelligence with science to
push the boundaries. And we've seen a good example
very recently, of course, with AlphaFold. So in that sense, from
a domains perspective, we honestly have
seen some examples already of narrow but
super intelligent system. AlphaFold was only doing that. And I think probably
that's the domains to think about where
we're going to start seeing super intelligence,
even from the general sort of capabilities
these models have. You might need to do
some specialization. And again, it might be worth it. Was it worth it to
solve protein folding? Absolutely, right? But I think that's
a good test to use. And we are very well positioned. Because we, of course,
have the whole science team and so on working on very
interesting problems. Now, if you take
the language models and you start
thinking about agents, putting them in
environments that could be more about science,
simulation, theorem provers, and so on, will
something very discrete be needed to enable
other breakthroughs? I would say probably not. Without another
transformer-like breakthrough, perhaps, feels like
we're going to start seeing more examples of, oh my
god, yeah, like in math now, it just discovers new
theorems that mathematicians find interesting. And it happened by just, of
course, very good execution plus scaling up of some of the
ideas and so on and so forth. HANNAH FRY: It is interesting,
though, that the first dominoes to fall are the ones which
have a ground truth-- ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: --like
science, as you describe. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. Although, yeah, science, it
depends which sciences might have ground truth, I suppose. HANNAH FRY: Protein
folding, definitely. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, it's true. I'm hoping we also
see some other ways to advance in a superhuman way. You could imagine having a
brainstorming scientific advisor that is powered by one
of these powerful models. And more than it
discovers something or it proves something new, it
just challenges your assumptions and it makes you think
out of the box in a way that, then, my creativity
sort of gets me to a place I couldn't have gone. Then you would call that
superhuman in some ways as well. So I think those are
definitely not out of scope and much harder to
also, of course, think of how do you
reward that behavior. HANNAH FRY: Absolutely
fascinating. There was definitely a lot
of drastic stuff in there. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah. Yes. HANNAH FRY: Thank you
so much for joining me. ORIOL VINYALS: Yeah, likewise. Thanks, pleasure. See you in five years. HANNAH FRY: [LAUGHS] I think
there was this real theme that emerged from that conversation,
at least for me anyway, which was this
idea of generality. And if you think about it, there
is this generality in the way that intelligence
advances knowledge. Like those old astronomers
like Copernicus, they were assessing lots of
data from observing the sky and using that to extract a
model of the solar system. But in the case of AlphaGo,
it was observing games of Go to extract a model for the
best possible way to play. And now, somewhere
embedded in everything that has ever been
created by humans is this model, this
underlying truth of how we experience reality. And the model that we're
looking for, of course, it's never going to be
as neat as heliocentrism. But that model does seem
to be in there, hidden among the frozen
weights of Gemini. Now, if that's what
we've done so far, the next phase is to try
and use those general ideas to extract a model of
human preferences, too. And that is, of course,
a lot, lot harder. But if we succeed, it might just
get us to a more general form of intelligence, too, AGI. Now, if you found this
conversation interesting, I think it's worth
also checking out the episodes I
did with Jeff Dean on, among other things,
scaling, and Iason Gabriel on the ethics of AI agents. Or if you want to dig a bit
deeper into the development of Gemini 2.0, then you can
check out the latest episode of the new "Google
AI Release Notes" podcast with host
Logan Kilpatrick. This and other
episodes can be found wherever you get your podcasts. Until next time. [MUSIC PLAYING]

HANNAH FRY: Hello, and welcome
to the Google DeepMind Podcast with me, Professor Hannah Fry. Now, those of you
who are watching will notice that we are
not in the studio today, and that is because we
are backstage at the AI for Science Forum, a
very special event that has been co-hosted between
the Royal Society and Google DeepMind. And as part of the day,
I had the opportunity to speak to Demis Hassabis
on stage, to interview him, and we thought that you might
like to hear that conversation. So we have included it
here as a special episode. And then even more
special than that, as though one Nobel
laureate wasn't enough, we have three further
Nobel laureates joining me on stage for an
extended panel for you to enjoy. [MUSIC PLAYING] OK, now for the next
session, it is always an absolute treat for me
and often a mind boggling experience to get to interview
your co-host for today, sir Demis Hassabis. Now, Demis is a
computer scientist. He's an Artificial
Intelligence researcher. He's an entrepreneur
who co-founded DeepMind in 2010, where he is still CEO. Now, I don't know how much you
know about Demis' background. Because as well as the Nobel
Prize, and the knighthood, and being a fellow of the
Royal Society, and AlphaFold, and AlphaGo, and having
150,000 citations, he also had a previous life as a
very successful neuroscientist, chess champion, and
video games designer. I have to say, I have a
theory that, actually, Demis worked out the secrets
to AGI a little while ago and has been hiding it in his
basement and then just, like, slowly eking out all of
these massive breakthroughs one-by-one to the rest of us. But one thing is for
sure, our conversations are always genuinely
fascinating. So please, welcome
Demis Hassabis. Thank you. [APPLAUSE] Thank you, Demis. Thank you so much. OK, I heard that you didn't know
that the Nobel Prize was coming. Is that true? DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, no-- HANNAH FRY: Really, really? DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: How
did you find out? DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, it was
quite a funny story, actually, because we had heard some
rumor that our fold had been sort of nominated,
but you never expect something like that. And in the morning, I was just
getting on with my normal work. Actually, my wife was
working at home, as well, and it got to about 10:30. And we thought,
oh, obviously, it's not happened this year,
because we hadn't heard. And then, suddenly,
my wife's computer started ringing on her
Skype, I think it was. And I said, what's
that annoying noise? And then it turned out
to be a call from Sweden, and they were desperately
trying to get hold of my number, and they didn't have
John's number either. So everyone was in
a kind of panic, but it all added to the
drama of the moment. Just 10 minutes before-- HANNAH FRY: You
had john's number? DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, exactly. Just before it
was all announced. HANNAH FRY: Absolutely
amazing, and I also saw that you celebrated
by having a poker night with some chess
champions, including Magnus Carlsen and Hikaru, as well. I want to know, who won? What's your bluffing strategy? [LAUGHING] DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, it turned
out-- yeah, so that happened. I think it was on the
Wednesday, and it turned out that there was a big, huge chess
tournament going on in London. And one of my chess
friends, old chess friends from when I was young, was
hosting a poker and chess evening the day after. So I thought it was the
perfect way to celebrate, but I don't recommend
a home poker game with a couple of
world poker champions and a couple of
ex-world chess champions if you want to win some money. But that's my idea of
fun, and it was actually an amazing night. I know it sounds pretty nerdy,
but it was actually my idea of heaven to celebrate it. HANNAH FRY: Not at all. It sounds like-- DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, I can't
tell you the bluffing strategy. Otherwise, I'll never
beat Magnus again. [LAUGHING] Yeah. HANNAH FRY: Well, this is true. This is true. I mean, aside from
the Nobel Prize, a sentence that no one has
ever said, before that, in advance of that, you also
were named as a citation laureates, because the AlphaFold
work has now been cited over 28,000 times, which
is incredible. I mean, this is really not very
long ago that it came through. Are there standout applications
for you that really resonate? DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, I mean,
it is mind boggling, really, and it's everything
we hoped would happen by putting AlphaFold out
there, and open sourcing it, and putting it out
with the community, with our fantastic partners at
EMBL-EBI, like Janet, and Ewan, and the audience as well, to
get it out there to everyone. I mean, there's so many
applications to mention, but I think maybe I can mention
two or three of my favorites were kind of determining the
structure of the nuclear pore complex, one of the most
important and largest proteins in the human body. And it's really
important, because it governs the molecules
and nutrients that go in and out of your cell nucleus. And, actually, several teams
used AlphaFold predictions, as well as their own
experimental data, to finally piece that
very, very complex protein structure together. So that was amazing. Another piece of work
that I really like is [INAUDIBLE] work
from the [? Broad, ?] developing a molecular syringe
to deliver drug payloads to hard parts of
the body to reach, and he also used
AlphaFold to help modify how that molecular
syringe was designed. And then maybe, finally,
my other favorite project is from the University of
Portsmouth, John McKeon's group, designing plastic eating
enzymes using AlphaFold. But I think it's
just a small sample of all the incredible work
researchers are doing with it. HANNAH FRY: But these
kind of projects, I mean, these are your
favorites, right? The ones where it's not just
sort of the technical idea itself, it's the
potential impact that it can have
further downstream? DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, that's right. I mean, that's why I was always
intrigued by the protein folding problem or protein structure
prediction problem was because I felt-- we sometimes call it a
deepmind, a root node problem. What we mean by that
is, if you think of the whole tree
of knowledge, there are certain problems, where if
there are root node problems, if you unlock them, if you
discover a solution to them, it would unlock a whole new
branch or avenue of discovery. And I always felt that
determining a protein structure in this way would do that,
lead to disease understanding, drug design, and
much more, and that seems to be what's happened. HANNAH FRY: OK, so not to
be greedy, but what's next? Is there another AlphaFold? I mean, GNoME is a
personal favorite of mine. Maybe tell us a little
bit about that project. DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, so GNoME,
look, there's so many areas, and actually, James
mentioned it in the morning, and Pushmeet talked
about it as well, that we're touching on
nearly every area of science. And GNoME is one of my
favorite projects, which is on material
design, and I think material design has some of the
same characteristics that we look for in a problem
that's suitable for AI. It's massive
combinatorial space. You need to try and
build a model that understands the physics
and the chemistry of the natural phenomena. And then, if you have
a model like that, you can then maybe use it to
do a very efficient search through that search space,
combinatorial space, and then find an
optimal solution. And in materials,
I think that would be also just as groundbreaking. You could imagine designing new
batteries or maybe, one day, discovering a room
temperature superconductor. It has always been
one of my dreams. So I think we're, obviously,
at the early stages of that. I would characterize it as sort
of AlphaFold one level maybe, and we've got to
get to AlphaFold two level of predictions. But we can see a
clear path from there, and GNoME is the
beginning of that work. We published it in-- I think it was in
Science, last year, and we discovered 200,000 new
crystals that no one had ever seen before. So I think that shows some of
the potential of AI in things, like material design. And then maybe the other
thing I'm excited about is AI applied to
mathematics and, perhaps, solving one of the
great conjectures, maybe one of the Millennium
prize problems, and using AI as a big
part of that solution. HANNAH FRY: So that idea,
though, about GNoME, also about AlphaFold, is
you're sort of shortcutting, or you're making the
sort of synthetic world really serve your advantage. So you're not having
to do everything through experimentation. I know that you
and Paul Nurse have been talking about a virtual
cell for a long while. Tell us a little bit about that. DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, Paul
has been one of my mentors. He's very generously mentored
me in the life sciences for more than 25 years
now or something, and it's been incredible,
sort of inspiring, talking to Paul very
regularly about these topics. And, unusually, I think Paul for
a life scientist, a biologist, he's thought a lot about biology
as an information system. He's written a lot of
interesting research papers on that. So we've always had this
discussion in the background, and I've always wondered-- every
five years or so, I've thought, do we have enough
technologies, enough know how to actually
really attempt this Mount Everest of a problem
or trying to build a virtual cell, a simulation
of a cell, basically, that would be predictive
of something that's really going to happen? And every five years, I
thought, we don't really have enough technologies yet. But, finally, now, I think the
answer is, yes, we probably do have enough know how and
techniques that we could attempt this seriously now. And maybe, in the
next five to 10 years, we could eventually
build up a picture of a virtual cell, perhaps, a
yeast cell to start off with, as Paul's always been working on
as the model organism for this. And my way of thinking
about it is you can think of AlphaFold2
as essentially solving the static picture
of what a protein looks like. But of course, we know
biology is a dynamic system. That's where all the interesting
things happen in biology, and AlphaFold3 is our
first step towards trying to model those interactions. So AlphaFold3 can model
pairwise interactions between proteins and proteins,
proteins and RNA, proteins, and DNA. And then maybe the
next step up from that would be to model
a whole pathway, and then, eventually, maybe we
can make it to an entire cell. HANNAH FRY: Amazing. I mean, I imagine that the
advent of quantum computing will make a difference now that
it's possible to do simulations down at the molecular level. DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, so
look, quantum computing is very exciting. It itself is accelerating,
and James also mentioned earlier
this morning about the interesting
cross-pollination that's happening between
AI and quantum computing. Actually, we collaborate a
lot with our quantum computing group at Google, which is one
of the world's best quantum computing teams, on things,
like error correction codes and things like that. And of course, one of the
uses of a quantum computer would be to simulate quantum
systems, like molecules, and atoms, and things
like that, and compounds, and then produce a lot of
synthetic data potentially. But, interestingly, I also have
a slightly controversial take, which is-- and I've talked to some of the
world's top quantum computer scientists on this,
is that, actually, I believe that classical Turing
machines, classical computers, are capable of a lot more
than we previously thought. And I think that's what the
work that we've been doing has shown, both with
AlphaFold, but also previously with AlphaGo,
our program that beat the world champion
at the game of Go, which just as an indication
of the complexity Go, much more
complicated than chess and has more possible
board positions than there are atoms in the
universe, 10 to the power 170. So what that means
is you can't possibly brute force a solution
to find the best move in a particular position. You have to do
something much cleverer. So both protein folding
also has enormous numbers of possibilities, if you were
to do it naively and just try every combination, which is
the naive way of doing it. But yeah, if you
do things, like you do a huge amount of pre-compute
to build a model of the system before you ask it the question
that you're interested in, what move should I play
in this go position, how does this novel
protein fold, it turns out you can actually come back
with a near optimal move in Go in a few seconds or fold a
protein in a matter of minutes, which you might have
expected in protein space, at least, that you might need a
quantum computer for or quantum algorithm for. And it turns out, you don't. So I actually think we should
take this very seriously, that classical systems, if
you use them in the right way, may be capable of modeling
a lot more complex systems, perhaps, even
counterintuitively, quantum systems. Because, normally, you talk
about needing quantum computers to model any kind
of classical system, but it may be that
classical systems can model quantum systems. And I've tested this out
with some of the people, like Professor Zeilinger, who
won the Nobel Prize in physics recently for his pioneering
quantum computing work, and he thought it
was very interesting. And David Deutsch, also,
one of my scientific heroes, who's basically invented quantum
computing, said it was crazy, but the right sort of crazy,
which coming from him, I take as a compliment
and as also a sign to maybe pursue it further. HANNAH FRY: So let me make
sure I understand this then. So, I guess, the
classical computers are thought of as
deterministic machines, and you're talking here about
probabilistic questions. But you can use the
deterministic ones. DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, and the idea is that quantum systems
are anything you try to mold, any natural phenomena. If you start trying to model
it by every possibility that it could take, that
system, then you quickly run out of computation
in a classical system. You need too many
bits to model it, but that's just not the way
that you would attempt to do it with a classical system. You would build a model,
first, that learns-- my conjecture on it would be
that any natural phenomena tends to have structure. And if it has structure,
you could potentially learn it with a classical
machine learning system, learn an efficient
model of that, and then use that to
search the possibilities in an efficient way. So I think that might get around
some of the inefficiencies from a naive way of doing this. I mean, it's a pretty big claim,
so I'm making it in a soft way. But it's a kind of hobby of
mine to look at this area, and it's something, I think,
that could be quite promising. HANNAH FRY: Another
hobby of yours that has now spun off into
its own business, Isomorphic, because, I mean, this
is work using AlphaFold to apply to drug discovery. DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes. HANNAH FRY: You've got some
pretty prestigious partnerships now as well. Can you tell us what Isomorphic
is focusing on at the moment? DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, so
Isomorphic is our spin out to try and revolutionize drug
discovery from first principles using AI from the ground up,
trying to re-imagine the drug discovery process. And I had this in mind when
we were doing AlphaFold. So I think it's one of the most,
obviously, good use cases of AI is to cure diseases. I mean, what better use
of AI could there be? So that has always been one
of the number one things I wanted to do with AI
once it got mature enough. So AlphaFold, of
course, is a great tool for fundamental research and
fundamental biology research. Over two million
researchers around the world have used AlphaFold and the
structures we put out there, but it's also a practical use
to help with drug discovery. Of course, knowing the
structure of a protein is only one small part of the
whole drug discovery process. So we spun out isomorphic
after we did AlphaFold two to build on that work and extend
and build new machine learning systems into adjacent
areas, things, like designing chemical
compounds and drug compounds, testing for toxicity
and predicting things, like ADME properties,
important properties that you need for drugs
to work in the body and minimize side effects
and things like that. And we're building-- you can
think of it as building up further AlphaFold-like models
in these adjacent areas. And then, eventually, we'll
stick them all together, and we hope that one day, in
the near future, actually, we'll reduce the time down
from years, maybe even decade, to design a drug down to
months or perhaps even weeks. And that would
revolutionize, I think, the drug discovery process. HANNAH FRY: This
really does seem like one of those
endeavors, where you're not having to make a choice between
doing something that benefits humanity and doing
something that can be profitable and sustain itself. How much do you look for those
kind of projects at the outset, or how much of it is that you
do sort of blue sky research and then hope that it'll
turn out that way in the end? DEMIS HASSABIS: I
think we do both. We're research led as a
group, always have been, so we try to do what's the right
next step for researching AGI, Artificial General Intelligence,
or something, like AlphaFold, where you really want to solve
the scientific challenge. But then, in the back of my
mind, I'm also pretty practical. So I want to solve
things that will also have a practical, positive
impact on the world pretty directly. So if you can find
projects that do both, then that's the
holy grail, really, and something that
really worthwhile, investing a lot of
time and effort. And AlphaFold was
exactly like that. So I was fascinated
by the problem itself. As Janet mentioned earlier,
the more you get into proteins, they're exquisite
bionanomachines. I mean, they're
unbelievably beautiful. I totally agree
with Janet on that, and you sort of fall
in love with them when you start working on them,
and it's just unbelievable what nature's engineered. But then I always had in mind
that would be a social good, but then Isomorphic
can do both, right? I think we can cure many
diseases with the help of AI, but also, it should be an
incredibly valuable company. And that, of course, if that
turns out to be the case, that will give us more money to
invest in fundamental research. So it's all a virtuous cycle. HANNAH FRY: With all of
these different projects going on in
fundamental research, I mean, do you think that we
are, at this moment, where we're going to have sort of a
vertical takeoff, as it were, on progress? DEMIS HASSABIS: I think we
are on the cusp of that. I really do feel like we're on
the brink of a new golden era of discovery, like the whole
of today's symposium is called. And I think what we
need is a lot more interdisciplinary science,
so using AI, bringing in the right way. We're asking the right
questions with domain experts, and I think it's
almost limitless, what its applications could be. And of course, AI itself
is a scientific discipline, is improving all the time. So there's applying today's
technologies directly to the other fields,
and then there's also continuing to improve AI
itself, and that as well is a sort of
exponential improvement. So there's a lot of,
I think, progress to be made in just
the next few years. HANNAH FRY: I think
interdisciplinarity has definitely been one of
the big themes of today, but I think that the other
themes are about what science looks like in the future. What does it mean to do
science in an era of AI? And I know that you're
a sort of big advocate for the scientific
method, but just talk to me a little bit about that. What does that look like
as we kind of move forward? DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, look,
I think the scientific method is arguably maybe the greatest
idea humans have ever had and, I think, underpins,
obviously, all of science, but then
also because of that, science and technology,
modern civilization. And I think more than ever,
we need to anchor around that method in today's world,
and I think, especially with something as powerful
and potentially transformative as AI, I think it's important we
use more the scientific method than, perhaps, lean on
normal sort of technology, A/B testing out in the wild. That is often the case
with new technologies, and I feel we should treat this
more as a scientific endeavor, if possible,
although, it obviously has all the implications
that breakthrough technologies normally have in
terms of the speed of adoption and the speed of change. So it's an interesting
situation that we're in, and I think we need to use the
scientific method to do things, like better understand what
these systems do, build benchmarks and
rigorous evaluations for understanding the
limits of the capabilities, interpretability. Actually, I think we should be
using neuroscience techniques, which are, obviously, built
for understanding real brains, to model virtual brains. I sometimes call it
virtual brain analysis. What's the equivalent of an fMRI
of these neural networks, right? And I think there's a lot we
can learn, and bring across, and translate from
the natural sciences to what is basically
an engineering science. I call it an
engineering science, because unlike the
natural sciences, you have to build the
artifact of interest, first, and then, once you
have it, you can then use the scientific method to
reduce it down and understand its components. So it's a whole challenge. That's for sure, and these
systems, in my opinion, are as complex as the
natural phenomena we normally want to study. So no one should think
that it would be easier to understand one of
these artificial systems than it would be to
understand a natural system. I think they're just as
complex in many ways. So we've got a lot of
work in front of us, and I think this is something
that industry, academia, and civil society needs to come
together to understand better, including how to deploy
these technologies. HANNAH FRY: Where does
a scientist's intuition fit in all of this? DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, I
think scientist's intuition and creativity is critical. I think, right now, the
AI systems are just tools. I think they're great
for finding correlations, and patterns, and structures
in data, but for the moment, they're not able to come up
with their own hypotheses or their own questions. And I think, as all the
scientists in the room know, I think that's the hardest
thing about science is asking the right
question, and that has to still come
from human scientists. And for the foreseeable future,
I think that will be the case. So I think it could
be-- that's why I'm very excited about the
use of these AI systems as maybe the ultimate
tools to help us accelerate scientific discovery. HANNAH FRY: I guess on that
topic of humans, I mean, there has been some
recent research saying that the progress of
scientific discovery has slowed down
over recent years. What's your take
on that, and what could be done to change that? DEMIS HASSABIS: Well,
look, I think things-- I've seen studies
like that, too, and I think there's interesting
conjectures as to why that is. Science has become
a bigger endeavor. You need to have bigger teams,
more expensive equipment, and so on. So that leads to some slowdown,
and perhaps, the questions we're now tackling
are ever more complex. So that's also tricky. I think what I
would recommend is, again, I think, a lot of the
advances in the next 10 years are going to be
interdisciplinary work, sort of bringing together experts
from two or more fields, and then that making the big
advances in between those areas. And, actually, that's the story
of DeepMind, which originally, I think, was a combination
of neuroscience ideas with machine learning ideas. It's also the
story of AlphaFold, which is the combination
of biologists and chemists on our team, along with machine
learning experts and engineers. So I've always found
that's the place to get the most
advances most quickly, and actually, we're announcing
today, a $20 million fund from Google.org
to fund this kind of interdisciplinary
work in academia. And I hope that other
funders join that effort, and I think that's
what we need is to train a new generation
of PhDs and postdocs in these kind of combination
of these different areas. HANNAH FRY: What an
extraordinary piece of news to finish on, $20
million for research in interdisciplinary teams. Demis, thank you
so much, indeed. Thank you very much. [APPLAUSE] OK, I think you go down to
join the other laureates, while we out the stage. Now, I should tell
you, actually, the laureates, the word,
laureates, by the way, comes from the
laurel wreath, which was given to victors in ancient
Greece as a sign of honor. And every year, there
are five separate prizes that are awarded to those who
are considered to have conferred the greatest benefit to
humankind courtesy of Alfred Nobel. There is a rumor that there is
no Nobel Prize for mathematics, because Alfred Nobel's
great love ran away with a mathematician. Have we heard this? I mean, we are charming. What can I say? Now, so joining me
on the stage, we have three further recipients
of this esteemed prize, John Jumper. We've not yet had the
pleasure of having John Jumper on the stage thus far. He is a director at
Google DeepMind, where he led the team that built
AlphaFold and continues to work on new methods
to apply machine learning to protein biology. John has won numerous
awards for his work, including the Lasker
Award, the Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences, the
Canada International Award, and of course, this year's
Nobel Prize in chemistry. We are also joined
by Sir Paul Nurse, who is CEO of the
Francis Crick Institute and winner of the 2001 prize
in physiology of medicine for his work also on proteins,
specifically the protein molecules that control the
division of cells in the cell cycle. And, finally, Jennifer Doudna,
who earlier shared her lessons from CRISPR with James, she won
the Nobel Prize in chemistry in 2020. Please, join me in welcoming
our four Nobel laureates to the stage. Thank you. OK, so I know that,
often, you get asked about where
were when you heard, what were you doing
when you heard. I want to ask you a
slightly different question. I want to ask you about
whether there was a moment when you realized that the
work that you were doing was genuinely groundbreaking. Was there sort of a
moment when you realized the significance of it? John? JOHN JUMPER: I think there were
kind of two moments for me. One was actually
watching Twitter after-- whenever you release
some work into the world, you anxiously refresh
Twitter, and we stick to the word AlphaFold, and
just see what tweets popped up. And I remember seeing,
when the database became available, so many
astounded grad students saying-- I think one was, how did
they get my structure, it hasn't been published,
how in the world, and just had
absolute astounding. Like, the number of
people that said either we predicted their structure,
this person down the hall, we predicted their structure, so
I thought they were absolutely blown away. And I think the
second moment was there was a special
issue of Science related to the structure of the
nuclear pore, the largest collection of proteins in the
human cell, protein chains, and there had been, like,
four papers in a special issue of Science, and
three out of the four had made huge
usage of AlphaFold. Something like more
than 100 mentions of the word
AlphaFold in Science, and we had nothing
to do with it. We didn't know it was happening. It just appeared
one day, and that was the moment when I really
knew that people were doing science worthy of appearing
in Science, one of the most prestigious journals in the
world, on top of our tools and without us. And the moment at which people
start making these discoveries on top of what
we've built, that's when you really make it as
someone who makes tools. HANNAH FRY: Jennifer,
did you have a moment? JENNIFER DOUDNA:
Well, I think for us, yeah, maybe also two moments. One was in the Fall
of probably 2011, when we had started a collaboration
with Emmanuelle Charpentier to work on CRISPR, which is
a bacterial immune system, and we wondered,
how does it work? And we had together figured
out that it's an RNA guided system that targets
DNA for cutting, for cleavage, and furthermore,
that we could actually program the system, because
we understood the chemistry of how it worked. And it was really one
of those aha moments. Personally, I was just astounded
that bacteria had figured out how to do this and that,
furthermore, we now understood how to harness that system to
manipulate DNA in new ways. So that was one, and
then the second one was sort of akin
to what you said, John, where I think it was then
fast forwarding about a year or so. We published that work in the
Summer of 2012, and by the Fall, I was starting to get
emails from people all over the world
who had read the paper and said, "Oh, my gosh,
this is so exciting. I'm now using it to test
genes in Drosophila. I'm starting to test
genes in zebrafish. I'm excited about what
I'm doing in human cells." I was getting messages from
people that I often barely knew who were just
very, very excited, and you could start to
feel the momentum that was building in the field. SIR PAUL NURSE: Cool. Well, I'm the oldie here,
so it's 1985, I'm afraid, and I work on yeast, as
you've already been told, and my lab had worked
out the genes that controlled the cell cycle. That is the process that leads
to the reproduction of one cell into two fundamental to growth
and development in all living organisms, and we'd
identified that gene. But the blunt thing is
that, who cares about yeast? I mean, being perfectly
honest, I care about yeast, but most of the world
does not care about yeast. So I found our work
wasn't really being taken much notice of. So I thought, do humans
have the same gene? Now, this is long before the
human genome sequence and so on. So we did a crazy experiment. I want to just tell
you how crazy it was. We had a mutant in yeast
that couldn't grow, because it was
defective in this gene. We took the first
human cDNA library. It's genES that
had ever been made. We didn't make it. We got it a few months
afterwards and fundamentally sprinkled the genes onto
the defective yeast, arguing that, if there
was a gene in humans that could do the same job as
the defective gene in yeast, if the yeast cell took it
up, if it could be expressed, if it worked, then those
cells would grow and divide, and we could get the gene back
and show that humans had it. That experiment had
no right to work. Yeast, humans probably diverged
1,500 million years ago, and we were demanding that it
still worked after 1,500 million years, and you
know, it did work. When we got that result-- and by the way,
there were a couple of months when we had
to sequence the genes. It took that long. Then I used to
think, I'm just going to go home and
believe this worked. Tomorrow, I will go
into work, and we will have shown it didn't
work, but it did work. And that's when I
thought, perhaps, it might be recognized,
because it wasn't yeast. It was humans. HANNAH FRY: What an amazing
collection of stories. Now, I know that all of you

40 years later, 50 years later, actually, more than

to believing that we can, OK, as president of
the Royal Society? You're good. The second question
from Roger there about intuition from AI systems? DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, look,
I think, actually, this is pretty interesting. I'm not so worried
about it as other people are, because I think
we're in a moment in time. So what I said earlier,
actually, my talk was what I believe, which
is that AI is an engineering science. So what it means is you have
to build the artifact, first, worthy of study, and then
you can break it down with a scientific method. So what you've seen
in the last five, 10 years is the building of the
artifacts that are even worth putting any effort into
study, until you have things, like today's transformer models
and AlphaFold's, AlphaGo's. The earlier systems
were probably not worth really putting
the effort in to study, because they were not
sophisticated enough. Now, they are. So now, people are seriously
studying the current systems. On top of that, you've
got the additional benefit of these systems improving
themselves, and at the minimum, I think, we'll be
in a situation, where, firstly,
the systems might be able to explain themselves
in language, or mathematics, or code. So we're getting close to that
as you could say to a system, OK, you've understood this. Now, explain that in a
mathematical equation to the extent that it can be. And I'm not sure biology
can be explained, like the laws of
physics, by the way. I think it is a lot messier. It's more to do
with interaction. So I think a simulation would be
more appropriate that you probe than, like, Newton's laws of
motion or something like that, and I don't think biology
could be reduced to that. It's too complex. And then the other thing
is what I also said earlier about applying neuroscience
techniques, analysis techniques to these
artificial neural networks, and we should be able
to get, at least, the same level of
insights into them as we do with natural brains. So if you combine
that together, we should get pretty far
already, let alone with further engineering
efforts that we're going to put on top to
decompose these systems. So I think, in the next five
years, we'll be out of this era that we're currently in
of kind of black boxes. HANNAH FRY: Fascinating. OK, further questions? All right, we've

Over to you, John. JOHN JUMPER: I mean,
should I answer this, or should I go to the-- HANNAH FRY: Yeah, go to the
attention, go to the attention. JOHN JUMPER: I think
attention is all you need is an oversimplification. I think one of the things
that's really interesting is AlphaFold isn't just,
like, grab a transformer off the shelf from
the transformer store and apply it to protein
structure prediction. It took a couple of years,
because there's a lot of work, and there's a lot of new ideas,
where attention is a component, but we have what we
would call evil former. We have new ideas on top. We have all sorts of-- Demis runs an
incredible AI research org, which goes into work every
day and doesn't just say, well, we've got all we need. There's a tension, right? We all are doing this
work, and it's really, I think, people
underestimate how much really novel and exciting

systems transformatively better, both unlocking new data
sources and learning a lot more from the existing data sources. AlphaFold is a story of having

about protein structure from it. So I think we'll continue to

something a tension. But it reminds me of this
old computer scientist joke. I don't know what the scientific

be called Fortran, right? [LAUGHING] So we tend to keep the labels of
ideas and then update within it. And I think we
