[APPLAUSE] SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:
This panel has to do with the
frontiers of science. And I've interpreted
and we've interpreted-- we had a conversation before-- the word frontiers very broadly. Obviously, one of the
frontiers of AI is the human. Another frontier
of AI is what is going to be done with
all the knowledge that is being collected and how that
knowledge influences discovery. So there's a question of
discovery being the frontier. And the management of all of
these incredibly powerful tools is also a frontier,
which leads us to the question of
what is a human. To some people, a human
is a locus of information. A human has personhood. We experience
grief, joy, sorrow. We are patients. We are caretakers of patients. We suffer when we have diseases. We suffer when our friends,
and family, and loved ones have diseases. We have privacy. We have dignity. We have empathy. And we are vulnerable. So there are many other
things, many other vectors. We also discover new things. And when we discover
new things, we are challenged, because
we have to figure out what to do with those discoveries. So the way I thought that
we would run this panel is to first talk,
for each one of us to talk about how we
use AI in discovery, in our particular discovery, to
be very specific about how we use AI in our discovery,
and a little perspective on how each of our
fields is working with the discovery and AI. The second aspect I
want to talk about is to veer a little
bit into controversy and ask the question--
because I think that it is almost inevitable
that, at least in my lifetime, that there will be some
version of an AI Fukushima. And by that, I
mean there will be a breach of data, or
a breach of privacy, or a discovery that takes us to
the brink of something that's unpalatable, that's dangerous. There's already been, in the
recent elections, the use of AI, not in our sphere,
but in the use of AI as a weaponized mechanism
of false information. So that brings us
to another frontier, which is the frontier of
how to take all of this and potentially mitigate
against these problems. So that's going to be the broad
overview of what we're doing. And then finally, we'll
talk more optimistically about the future in
each of our fields, and talk about
what we think about as these fields move ahead,
and how we can incorporate while mitigating the risks. So I'll start with-- I'll call everyone
by their first name if that's OK, because there are
too many honorifics that follow. I'll start with Anne. And maybe, we can start with
you talking about what you do, and how you incorporate
AI in your work, and what the field
is looking at. ANNE VINCENT-SALOMON:
OK, thank you. Yes, my work is to
be a pathologist. But I am also the head of a new
institute about women's cancer. So first, starting
with pathology, AI-- we have been validating
or co-developing tools with different startups
to have tools enhanced to facilitate the diagnosis and
also to challenge a question. Can we predict a BRCA1 and
2 status from H&E slides? This was done with
an academic lab. Head being Thomas Walter
from the École des Mines PSL University. And so finally, I
found it very exciting, because it opens new
tools for diagnosis. It brings us speed,
but it has to be very accurate and efficient. The tool has to be
accurate and efficient. But at the end of the day, I
think that the human guarantee, because it will be so far
as I can forecast, always a pathologist who will sign
out, it is a breast cancer. But in the Institute of
Women's Cancer, which is an institute built by
Institut Curie, PSL University, and Inserm, we really want
to make new algorithms alone or with industry. And we hope to bring new tools
that will facilitate our lives. But maybe, we will
speak after about how we are going to make that happen
and accepted by the patients. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE: Sure,
we'll come back to that. Janet? JANET THORNTON: So
I'm Janet Thornton. I'm a computational biologist. And I've worked in the
world of protein structures. And I don't know
if you saw at lunch that wonderful display of a
protein structure rotating. To me, they are some of the most
beautiful things in the world. And so I started when we only
had 20 protein structure. With AlphaFold, now, we can
model 214 million protein structures. So the change in the field-- so
if we're asking what impact will AI have on science-- in this case, it had the
most enormous impact, so that now structural
biologists worldwide use this data to actually help
them solve their structures and to validate
their structures. And it's really
transformed the field. Recent progress, beyond
the original AlphaFold, has meant we can
look at multimers and look at ligand binding. And of course, we know that
proteins, which we're all made of, actually
only work because they interact with other things. And so being able to look at
and predict these interactions, and the modulations that
happen during evolution, to me, remains one of the most
exciting and demanding areas of molecular biology. But going on to the
cellular biology and understanding the human
body as a whole, and medicine in particular, then
we will need to go to this next step of integrating
the data for an individual cell and working out how that cell
interacts with other cells. And that, I think, will be an
enormous thing going forward. Actually, it's the basis of our
future understanding of life. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE: Great. Anna, tell us about what you do. ANNA GREKA: I think that's a
wonderful segue into my story. So I'm a physician scientist
and a cell biologist. And of course, the 37 trillion
cells or so in our bodies are really-- it's
the unit of life. And so understanding cells-- I mean, I actually
study membrane proteins. So proteins fascinate me
by how they come together to organize themselves in cells
and all the basic functions that basically allow us all to work,
our systems and organs to work. So I'd like to
illustrate how we use AI, by telling you a story
of scientific discovery that comes from my group. So I work at the
Broad Institute. So it's a place where a lot
of genetics and genomics happen on a daily basis. And so as a physician
scientist, I've taken an interest in trying
to understand how misspellings in the DNA lead to broken
circuits inside cells, and then, of course, how
we find ways to fix them. So this particular story begins
on the Mediterranean island of Cyprus, where it was
actually known for many decades that there were many families,
in which in every generation, several members
suffered and died from, what turned out to be,
a mysterious kidney disease. So the mystery was solved by
the Broad Institute in that we actually found that this was
because of a misspelling that was hiding in a very dark
corner of the human genome in a variable number
of tandem repeats region of a gene
called mucin 1, which is a glycoprotein,
a membrane protein. And then, my team took on
this challenge of figuring out how this misspelling
results in broken circuits inside these kidney cells. And in order to do that-- and
that's the first example of how machine learning was used,
and this is now for image recognition, essentially-- I have calculated, because I
had to tally up all the work that we did. We used a lot of
imaging-based approaches, as cell biologists do. But we did it at
high throughput. And I think, over the course
of several years, we imaged, what I have counted to
be, about 15 million cells that we basically were
able to then assess how these circuits
inside the cells are broken, on account of
this particular mutation. And then, of course, we also
discovered a small molecule by doing this approach--
again, image-based screening-- in order to,
hopefully, get to a therapeutic. And so what we
found in doing this, and this takes us to
the next use of AI, is that the misspelling
in the DNA, the mutation, results in a
misshapen protein that accumulates inside the cells. And this accumulation is
caused by another molecule, what I call a nodal molecule. It turns out to be something
called a cargo receptor. The details don't matter. But just to say that this is a
system that captures misshapen, misfolded proteins and
holds on to them so tight that the cells eventually
die, because there's too much accumulation of
toxic waste, if you will. And then it turns out that
this discovery is actually what I call a nodal
mechanism, because it's not only relevant to this particular
misfolded protein that causes kidney disease, but it
turns out, as we discovered, it's also relevant to a
misfolded protein that causes a form of Alzheimer's,
and a misfolded protein that causes a form of blindness
in the eye, and on and on, in the liver and the lung. So it turns out to be a nodal
mechanism that we can now target therapeutically for the
benefit of many more patients than we previously
thought possible. And how did we define
the perfect fitting drug, if you will, for this
particular nodal target? Well, AlphaFold-- and this is
a structure that I shared with Demis when we met last summer-- was actually brilliant
in figuring out how a heterotetrameric
structure, so four things coming together, are actually
forming a structure that creates a specific pocket
for a drug that we discovered that is now
disrupting this nodal mechanism and can, therefore,
have therapeutic benefit for all the diseases
that I mentioned. And I'm pleased to say that,
by using machine learning for images and then ultimately
AlphaFold for this target, we both did target discovery and
then drug discovery that is now actually making its way
toward patients in partnership with a pharmaceutical
partner from my lab. And so this is just an
example to illustrate the power of these tools
that can only get better and better as the years go on. And so I'm very excited for what
the potential is for the future. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:
So pathology, genomics, basic discovery-- I lie at the far end. I'm a drug discoverer
and a clinical scientist. I have a quick,
fun story as well. My lab has been involved in
making antibody drug toxins. These are antibodies that
are conjugated to toxins. One of the things that
we use, of course, is AlphaFold to figure out
the structure of the antibody and where to potentially
attach the toxins. And a very common drug in women
with Herceptin-resistant breast cancer is a drug called ENHERTU. It's been out for a while
now, very commonly used. The problem with ENHERTU is
that it causes a 10% to 20% risk of Interstitial
Lung Disease, which we didn't know why. And 2% to 3% of those
patients with ILD will die. And this is how
sad and, of course, how desperate the field is that
people will take a 2% to 3% risk of death in order to
get the benefit of ENHERTU. So with using AI tools, we were
making these various drug toxin combinations and putting
them into models to show ILD. And we asked the
AI module basically to make all combinations,
so to create every possible combination. And one combination it
threw out, which we said was a stupid idea, it
threw out the combination of just the antibody
with just the linker. And it said, "you haven't really
tested that part, have you?" And we said, well, that's
a really stupid idea. Of course, there's nothing. It's a linker. A linker is a passive element. It's the piece that
links the chemical-- to the toxin to the antibody. Well, as case may be, it turns
out that, in fact, the linker is the toxic part. We're just publishing that data. And we would have
not found that. So here's an example in
which the AI module suggested an experiment that humans had
to ingest and, basically, be humbled by and then find
out that, in fact, that was the right experiment to do. It was a control
that a smart graduate student would have suggested. We just neglected
to have done it. So across the spectrum, again,
an incredible use of AI. Let's talk about data, because

situation is really different within

glass slides from tissues that are biopsies or samples from

wouldn't have AlphaFold, right? JANET THORNTON:
--no, we wouldn't. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE: PDBE-- JANET THORNTON: I should
have made that point. Without the data, we won't
have any AI or any powerful AI. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:
--so what was it? JANET THORNTON: So in fact,
the International Union of Crystallographers
passed a statement saying that unless people
deposited their data, they would not be able to
publish in various journals. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:
It was not a carrot. It was a stick. JANET THORNTON: Well,
but there was also-- yes, it was basically a stick. But many of the community
were already on board. But there were some
outstanding exceptions. Some of the best people actually
didn't want to share their data. And so there needs to
be a change in culture for the medical data within
the clinical profession, actually, that
they see the value. And in terms of privacy,
that we can reassure people that the tools we have for
anonymization or pseudo anonymization are really
powerful, because that is often the most challenging thing. I think also, the
Worldwide Protein Data Bank is publicly available
for everyone. The data is essentially free. And I think this
brings, personally, even for medical
data, I would suggest that this-- because if you
have the knowledge of a given mutation and how to treat
it, that's knowledge. You publish it. And you want to share that
data as far as you can. So I would make a strong
plea for public data, even if it's taken you five
years or more to determine it, that there comes a
time when you can, and I think, should, because
most of the academic data-- it's different if
it's in a company that you can decide we
will collect this data. And this is our data. But actually, if
it's publicly funded, I think most people would agree. And I think here in the UK,
because we have our National Health Service, there's
a terrific opportunity if we can harness that data. And there's something called
Health Data Research, which is trying to promote and
facilitate the sharing of data with the NHS. And we have our secure
data environments in each hospital now. So this is a long process. As I've said, in the
crystallographic world, which is a tiny little world
actually, it took 20 years to do this for the
health world, which is infinitely bigger and
infinitely more complex. It's just going to
take a long time to set up this ecosystem
of data resources and access that allows
people to access it. But this is all with the

many hospitals and everything, really get together. Because I do believe
for infrastructure, it's kind of top down. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE: Right. JANET THORNTON:
Research is top down. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:
Anna, yeah, I had a particular question. But take it-- ANNA GREKA: I just wanted

to get enough perturbations of human cells in a systematic

a situation where all of us, including myself, have generated
millions of cells worth of data, single cell data, other kinds

in a way that would be most meaningful, in the

set in order to do that. And I, for one, would love

data set, analyze the data set, and really learn something
more about the unit of life, which is our cells. SIDDHARTHA MUKHERJEE:

self-referential silos, quite often the case. And we have to begin by
seeing how we can break down those silos, how we can
actually get the different parts of the scientific community

the case with respect to artificial intelligence,

the permeability to it, so it doesn't become a
sort of new priesthood that is somehow
separate from the rest of the scientific endeavor. So there are a number
of, what I might say, sort of social science

people at the heart. The phrase we use

well-understood disciplines. And so a lot of our base load

allow you to use AI for really critical

the diversified nature of delivering it. I mean, science is an activity. It's the pursuit of truth. It relies on evidence, on
data, on reasoned argument. Mr. Trump, remember all of those
things in the coming weeks, please. But it is different in
the ways that it operates, depending where it is on
the spectrum of discovery versus application. And that, sometimes, is missed
by our political masters. They can mix it all
together, understandably, because discovery is
anarchic, as I said. Applications are more directed. And that leads to something
that's more bottom up versus something more top down. Sharing the ways
in which they work, but also operating in
somewhat different ways. What else is
commonality is they all require excellent leadership,
both scientific and managerial, not necessarily always
in the same person. But we need both. What we don't want

business people trying to run it or the reverse. Secondly, we need
proper finances. We haven't talked about that. And that's really important. What I've called proper
end to end funding, that is looking
at the total cost of a research activity,
rather than simply a more limited direct cost. And that's important. And thirdly, which
reflects partly how I started, the diversity
of different organizations that we need to actually
produce the whole spectrum of activities. We have great
universities, which are producing things in a
completely anarchic way, often, but exceptionally important. We have great institutes,
which can sometimes be rather unfocused,
like the institute I run, or very focused, depending
on what you want to deliver. And then, of course, you
have commercial activities, which are bound to be more
focused, because they're trying to deliver. And we all have to be
tolerant of one another, permeable with one another,
and actually delivering all that endeavor. The danger comes when we think
there's only one way to do it within that spectrum. And the key word is the
diversity and approach across the board. ILAN GUR: Hear, hear. LILA IBRAHIM: Since I asked
Angela a personal question, if you don't mind my asking,

to teach something. I mean, it's just

things similarly, and introduced turnover longer
than 3 to 5 years, but for the majority, younger

for principals like me, which means you introduce turnover

practices which really do make things work well. LILA IBRAHIM: Yeah, there is. I had a mentor who used

for our model, there is something really

people, open to new ideas? And when we decided-- I think, surprisingly,

example-- to interface with the nervous system
at a different level. We've learned recently that a
lot of neurological disorders are circuit level
disorders of the brain. And when you think
about neurotech, it's all sort of about an
insertion of electrodes into one part of the brain. So Jacques, our
program director, has been thinking about
are there elegant ways to interface with the neural
system at the circuit level and be able to
create therapeutics and therapies for these
broad range of conditions. Initially, he really was pointed
towards having technologies that were low impact, in
terms of a real intervention. And what he learned was there's
very little patient data on this. So the interesting
question is, which is the bigger issue
for a patient, is it wearing a
helmet on your head all day long to get
your brain signals, or is it a one hour
outpatient procedure? And this is a really
interesting, tough question. And so as part of
that program, we're actually funding
some social science and some patient engagement
to think about that and feed back into the
research we're doing. LILA IBRAHIM: Great. And Paul, trust, accountability? PAUL NURSE: Yeah,
I think we've got to think about how we
connect with the public, not the experts, not those
who understand it, not necessarily the NGOs who are
talking about it, the public. And there, I think deliberative
democracy has a role to play. And I'm reminded of the
fiasco around GM crops, for example, when
nobody who was actually trying to introduce GM crops
had really properly talked to Joe and Joanna Public. And I was involved
with the Royal Society, actually, with some
deliberative, democratic experiments, really at
the time, just talking to those off the street,
explaining what the issues are and then getting the feedback. What was interesting about that
was the main answer we got about why they didn't like GM
crops-- it will become relevant in a moment-- was they didn't want to
eat food with genes in it. Now, that is something that a
scientist would never think of, of course. But that's what bothered them. And yet, of course, nobody was
actually dealing with this. So when we're thinking about
artificial intelligence and everything around
it, let's go out, talk to Joe and
Joanna Public and ask, what do they think about it at
this moment, what worries them, and then think about how
we communicate with them. If we don't get
down there, we'll find all those
who've got opinions and all those who have an

with the public. LILA IBRAHIM: So meeting the

realized is ARIA'S mandate is not just to do interesting
science that leads to technology breakthroughs, but to have those
breakthroughs radically benefit the future of the UK. And the question
becomes, well, OK, we're excited in our programs
about generating these catalytic counterfactual
research breakthroughs. When one of them
arises, how is it going to get out into the world? And our hypothesis is
that the biggest driver for that translation
will be people who are driven to see that impact. And so we think a lot about
science entrepreneurship. And so we've created

with people outside of us as a government

commercialization, and translation,

us in all of these spaces to inject talent, and
skills, and resources. So DeepMind is one of our

everything we're doing. But we have a number of others. And they really are
raising ambitions and finding people who
want to drive this impact and help them move forward. LILA IBRAHIM: Great. Paul? PAUL NURSE: I'm a biologist. I want to know what life is. That's my central mission. And I think we will
understand that. And we heard it from
the previous panel in understanding

in being able to put those things together. But we have to move on from

experiences in this area. I started out with John Donne. No man is an island. A lovely quotation, but

sure that we don't repeat the mistakes of
the past, especially with science and technology? And how do we make

I think when it comes to AI that brings this

to create good social outcomes? BOSUN TIJANI: Absolutely. Thank you so much, Paul. I think I have to
start by appreciating Tom for the picture you
painted, because this is something that
became my reality when I became Minister in Nigeria. The fact that when you look at
science ranking of countries in the world, and you compare
this to economic development and growth, you can
see direct correlation, literally direct correlation. But you find that
in Global South, you don't see strong
application of science in actually trying to address
many of the challenges that we face. You talked about high
dimensional problems. I think I saw [INAUDIBLE]
problems as well in my country and quite a
number of countries in Africa. These are problems where
we think we already have the answers to them. But the reality is that the
answers and the requirements for addressing these problems
are constantly shifting, so you can't necessarily
take that answer that we think we have on. For instance, if you take how
the UK managed to get to a point where quality of education is at
where they would like it to be, that's not something you can
transplant to Nigeria, a country that's adding five million
new people every year, literally with certain
spaces that you can't govern, because those spaces
are just not connected. So you have to be thinking of
new ways of actually solving that problem. And even what we knew 10, 15
years ago about fixing education in Africa is not
the solution today. And for me, I think that's where
science is extremely important. And whilst we may not
have invested enough in building the
base for science, I think artificial
intelligence offers that unique opportunity
for us to now be able to get more
insights and be able to process these
insights in manners that can hit how government is
looking to solve these problems. But the challenge
we have despite that is the fact that we're
living in a world where the development of AI
in itself is quite skilled, and we understand nations
have invested over the years. So the challenge
is for me, how do I accelerate government
efforts to ensure that whilst the investment
may not be there, that the things that we
need to do at the minute to quickly accelerate how AI
can help us through development, that we don't leave them behind? And I find three
things that I think are extremely important that
the world should actually pay attention to when it
comes to AI and Global South. And the first one for me
is inclusion in data set [INAUDIBLE]. If we have this fantastic
opportunity around how science can help us do what
Tom was talking about with the farmers in India,
but the data set that we have is not really inclusive
enough to actually tell us the reality of countries like
Nigeria or Ghana or Rwanda, then we're going to limit
how science can actually help to solve these [INAUDIBLE]
problems, which we all need to address. Because as much as Europe
may be aging in population, countries in Africa-- my country, the
average age is 16.9. This country is growing at
five million people a year. That's going to be one of the
sources of talent in future. The workforce of the future
will come from Africa, so we need to help strengthen
that, in my own opinion. So I think that gap in
inclusion in AI data sets will limit how artificial
intelligence can support science to help us accelerate
development. The second one, which
I think is important, and we all talk
about infrastructure, is also the fact
that infrastructure will lead to two things
from what we're seeing. One is agitation for
data sovereignty, which means countries
will start to say, we want to be able
to keep our data. We don't want you to collect,
harvest, and take them away. And what we get
with that is also the limitation that
will bring to helping us to strengthen AI to be able
to do what we want it to do. And the other part is also
the science in itself-- the development of science and
how it affects economic growth is not just linked to
what is done elsewhere and what you bring in. It's also the ability
of local scientists to be able to participate. So if they don't have access
to this infrastructure, it means we cannot also
encourage local scientists to actually do context-based
research activities that can help to also lead development. So that's something I find
quite troubling that I've been also been working. And the third part for
me is also the fact that a lot of what
is known about us is not coded in a manner
that AI can actually use. So we have too much dark data
on a lot of Global South nations that we need to be
looking for smart ways to digitize what is known so
that we can make them available, and they can help us deepen
the quality of most models that people now use to come up
with solutions to challenges. So I think these three
things broadly are things that I'm worried about. What we've done is to not
focus on the limitations of our reality at
the minute, but also say to ourselves that we
live in a connected world. Nigeria as a country has a
strong diaspora community, so we actually use an AI model
to find all Nigerians working in artificial intelligence. We took all the
publications, I think, for the last 30
or so years in AI, and we use a name
library to find those. It's extremely accurate. So we brought about
100 of them to Abuja to actually help us craft
our national AI strategy. We took in civil society
folks, researchers locally, and they worked together for a
week to build that foundation, and that foundation is now what
we're building on as a nation to shape the direction of
artificial intelligence in the country. The other thing
we're doing as well is we're now building what
we call the AI Trust, which is more of building a community
of trustees, folks who can support how AI
develops in the country, not just leaving it to the
capacity of government, because this is also a big
issue in Global South countries. Most governments are
playing catch up with AI. If you come to Europe, even
in countries where you may not have the leadership
from government, you have academic
institutions that are articulating where society
should be going around the AI. So government, in a way,
is playing catch up. But I think we're getting there. There's more conversation
and inclusion in conversations like this. But whilst we may still be
far from actually ensuring that we can include
those voices, help understand this reality,
and collectively start to partner towards
addressing them, I think we're still far
from where we should be. But we're on the journey. PAUL HOFHEINZ: Well,
that's fascinating. You mentioned the
question of talent, including the enormous
number of young people, 16 years old, that
you have in Nigeria. And I know from speaking to
you ahead of this meeting that you're making
efforts to attract talent to Nigeria as a competence

=== VIDEO: Bu-7NXtLqMo (https://youtu.be/Bu-7NXtLqMo) ===


THOMAS L. FRIEDMAN:
Thanks so much. It's a treat to be here
with my friends at Google and all of you. And I just came from
Jerusalem, so I am much more in the mind of AQ, covering
AQ, which is al-Queda, not AI, but I made the shift
on the plane ride up. To understand both
the potential of AI driving more scientific
breakthroughs and more scientific breakthroughs
driving more AI, I think you have to
appreciate the unique moment where humanity finds
itself right now, on the eve of this discovery. We've hit three scaling laws, it
seems to me, at the same time. A scaling law in
silicon and software, a scaling law in carbon, and
a scaling law in disorder. Let's briefly look at all
three and how they're related. At some point between 2017 when
Google released its transformer algorithm and the early 2020s
when microchips became that much more powerful and specifically
tailored to AI parallel processing, artificial
intelligence of a wholly different scope and
scale just emerged. I mean, the machine just
started speaking German, and they didn't teach it German. So obviously, there was a
scaling law playing out. The power of large
language modules that rapidly became, or are
becoming large reasoning modules now, emerged and
became available, we all know, to the
public, virtually for free. For the first time, polymathic
artificial intelligence seemed within sight,
and we quickly saw, with amazing
breakthroughs like AlphaFold, how scientific challenges
that took decades could be compressed
in a few years. AI-driven science went in three
years from science fiction to nonfiction to available for
everyone, virtually for free. At roughly the same
time, though, we had a scaling law in carbon. By that, I mean CO2 and methane
greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere
caused primarily by humans burning fossil fuels-- and cows belching, we
learned this morning-- that trap more heat
near the Earth's surface were causing destructive
climate change. Each of the last
three decades have been hotter than
the previous one, and the seven warmest
years on record actually occurred
between 2015 and 2021. The outcome is both
a hotter planet and a cascade of
feedback loops, impacting our oceans, atmosphere, soil,
rocks, trees, and every living thing on the planet. The net effect is what
my friend Hunter Lovins has called Global Weirding. The hots get hotter,
the wets get wetter, the droughts get
drier and longer, and destructive forest
fires and storms become more frequent
and more destructive. We see once-in-100-year weather
events becoming once every few years. I would argue that at
roughly the same time we hit these scaling points
in silicon and carbon, we hit a third scaling
point or tipping point, and it was in the challenges in
both developed and developing world of creating stable
and decent government. We're hitting a scaling
point in disorder. The central crisis for the
industrial democracies is that they have promised their
people health benefits, retirement benefits, and
infrastructure that they simply cannot deliver, while they are
also having to fight more wars against non-democracies. But today, they can
neither raise taxes enough, cut benefits enough,
or go into debt further without risking
political instability or runaway inflation. And all of this is happening as
populations in these countries age. In 2010, for instance,
1 in 10 Singaporeans were age 65 and above. By 2030, it will be 1 in 4. Also, educational attainment
is stagnating despite higher education spending. We've also seen a
broad-based slowdown in global labor productivity
growth over the last decade. In the US, Social Security,
Medicare, and Medicaid are now half the federal budget. Compounding all of this are
bigger national security challenges as the four
decades of globalization and the relative absence
of great power conflict, known as the post
Cold War, fades away. Meanwhile, the developing
world, precisely because of climate
change and deforestation and accelerations in
technological change and a lot less foreign aid
from the developed countries, too many nations are
finding it hard to deliver even the basics of education,
electric power, transportation, and health care. This is leading to more
internal migration and then external migration and more
failing states and conflict. According to the
Global Peace Index, there are currently 56 conflicts
going on in the world today, the most since World War II. And there are more migrants
moving from one country to another than any
time since World War II. For all of these reasons,
ladies and gentlemen, we need a game changer. What I've just described are
high-dimensional problems that blend both
scale and complexity. Only the kind of
high-dimensional thinking that AI-fueled scientific
discovery can address-- only the kind of
high-dimensional thinking that AI-fueled scientific
discovery can address is going to mitigate
these problems. Specifically, we
need more powerful AI driving more
scientific breakthroughs so that for less money
with greater ease, we can deliver better health
care, more decent government to more people
with cleaner energy than we have ever done
before as a species. The future stability of
this planet for our children depends on that. To put it another way,
we need the scaling that hits silicon and
software to produce artificial intelligence to
mitigate the scaling of carbon into destructive climate change
while also propagating the scale solutions we need to scale
decent governments to prevent scaling disorder. Can polymathic
artificial intelligence give us the polysolutions
for the polycrisis? That's why I call
this era the polycene. I know it's a mouthful. Here's the short version. Politics has never
needed science more. It has never needed
science to be an amplifier for the economy
by reducing costs in research and in areas like medicine
and sustainability, as well as increasing
productivity and workforce participation. Politics has never
needed science more to enable leapfrogs in
education and job growth for the developing world. And politics has never
needed science more to provide the buffers against
disruptive climate change. I'm an optimist, both by nature
and from my own reporting. I just give one example. I wrote a book in 2011 with my
friend Michael Mandelbaum called "That Used to Be Us." It was actually about America,
how America lost its way in a world it invented. And my favorite chapter in that
book was called "Average is Over". And the theme of the
chapter was, basically, with the acceleration in the
pace of change in technology, if you just had
average skills, it would produce an average income. So you could buy
an average house and have 2.0 average kids
with an average yard, average number of
trips to Disney World, and average retirement, and
a perfectly average funeral. Average was over. So everybody had to
find their extra. Well, I've been thinking
about that chapter lately because recently,
on a trip to India, I discovered a really
rudimentary AI system that was being provided
to Indian farmers, where an illiterate Indian farmer
could access on his cell phone this software-- his or her cell
phone this software-- and get the absolutely
world-class best advice on what seeds to plant, when to
plant them, where to plant them, which fertilizer,
how much water, which season based on everyone
in the world who was planting those seeds at the same time. For the first time
in the world, people who had never had
access to average are going to have
access to world-class in a whole range of
opportunities and outcomes, not just agriculture. Average is over for them in a
really cool way thanks to AI. So that's why I am an optimist-- with one minor caveat. While politics has never
needed science more, science has never needed
politics and ethics more. It's never needed
them to come up with the controls,
the regulations that will ensure that we are able
to get the best out of AI without becoming its pet. The alignment
problem is very real and will become
more real every year we get closer to true
artificial general intelligence. Have no illusions. With our invention of artificial
intelligence, we as a species have become more godlike
than ever before. We humans invented
a bigger brain than we evolved with, or for
the believers in the room, than God endowed us with. We humans now have the godlike
power to part the Red Sea. But unless we can scale to
each other and our AI machines the Ten Commandments
to go with it, we will have a real
problem on the other side. Thank you very much. [APPLAUSE]

across the fields beyond biology, in every field,
we are seeing a transformation. James mentioned our work on
weather prediction, GraphCast. Just to give you
an idea, GraphCast makes predictions about
near term forecasting. It used to take supercomputers
simulations for days to come up with a
prediction like that, and now, it can be done on
a single chip in a minute. That's like multiple orders of
magnitude sort of difference. The same thing in materials. There are around 30,000
inorganic crystals, which we knew were stable in our
work on graph neural networks for material exploration. We showed that there were 2.2
million inorganic crystals, which were considered stable,
that scientists did not know about, and we shared
400,000 of them last year. And scientists now
have discovered that many of the candidate
materials that we had listed are candidate materials for high
temperature superconductors, for better battery design
materials, and so on. So in every area
of science that we are looking at, whether it's
biology, structural biology, genomics, protein
design, it's just transforming what is out there. ERIC TOPOL: No question. I mean, Pushmeet

are two sort of elements to it. One is AI systems as tools, and
they are very powerful tools. They can make some things-- they can make predictions,
which are quite unique. And as was mentioned by
Alison, it's really important to understand when they
are making progress, and when they are correct,
and when they are wrong. And I think the real
breakthrough of AlphaFold was not just that it was
very accurate in predicting the structure of proteins. It was also that it was very
accurate in saying, I did not make the right prediction,
when it did not make the right prediction. So the uncertainty, the
predictive uncertainty of AlphaFold was
extremely well calibrated, and that gives confidence to
people who are using these sophisticated tools, that
they know when the tool can be trusted and when the
tool cannot be trusted. Now, this is not the case for
all the machine learning models out there. In the case of LLMs,
LLMs hallucinate, and one of the
grand challenges is that you don't know when
they have hallucinated and when they have come up
with something remarkably intuitive and profound. So one element of
this is, as you are using these
powerful tools, how do you build the scientific

these tools to the most effective manner. Then there comes to a point,

fusion reactors, and they are stabilizing plasma
configurations in a way that has not been designed by hand. It has been learned. So people who are
working in those systems, they are extremely
sort cognizant of the safety implications. They want to understand what
is the out of distribution generalization behavior
of these models. So validation, again,
is extremely crucial. When you give autonomy
to such a system, you need to understand what
are the limitations, how do you sandbox it, how
do you properly test it, how do you exhibit. Like, how do you test
all the different ways that systems can go wrong? So I think, both from
a tooling perspective, as well as from

challenges that we are actively sort of seeing the whole

point is extremely valid, right? If we are thinking about
developing intelligence, intelligence does not
happen, arise from a vacuum. It arises from
some raw material, and that raw material
is experience. Now, that experience could
be in the form of data that has been collected
by scientists in a lab, or that is experience
that the model has achieved by interacting

the model a lot of intuition about what is going to happen. Like, in the case of fusion
and many of our physics models, we do use a lot

the work that we have done in AlphaMissense,
which is also extremely sort of crucial. There is the third
element, which is synthetic data, which is
where you are trying to augment these data sources
by making predictions and distillation as a
concept, where you try to make predictions
about unlabeled data and then use those labels
to train a model in the hope that some of the
right predictions will give you intuitions,
and the wrong predictions will be thrown out. But that sometimes can
be wishful thinking, and it's really important

like, the Critical Assessment-- CASP is Critical Assessment
in Structure Prediction. And for those of
you who don't know, I think it was an amazing
and very visionary idea, where it was run
sort of biannually, and the competition was that
any sort of new structure that was experimentally found out
for a specific period of time, scientists would
deposit it in a vault and will not share
it with the world. And these sort of structures
were then kept hidden, and the whole community of
structure prediction, people who are working on structure
prediction would be told, OK, here are the things
that you should test on. And now, nobody
knows the answer, but the true answer
is no, right? So there's no way to cheat. So that gives you a very clean
signal of how much progress has been made, and you
are not fooling yourself by just overfitting or trying to
sort of think that you're making progress, but you're not. ERIC TOPOL: Yeah. FIONA H. MARSHALL:

reasoning at different levels, there is reasoning at
a non-expert level, and there was a
dramatic progress that AI made in things,
like perception. That's not something
that an expert-- like, we all can, basically,
perceive the world and do can do object detection,
image classification. But it was a big
breakthrough when AI systems were able to do it. They were able to achieve
non-expert level reasoning behavior. Then there is expert
level reasoning behavior, where you have a person
who has spent a lot of time in understanding
a particular field and getting the real
sort of facts out. And then there is beyond
expert level behavior, which is what systems, like
AlphaFold and AlphaMissense, do, where there are no humans that
are able to solve that problem. So I think, if these models
are able to do these reasoning problems from the
input that they have, if there are no
signals or there are no input features that
they can sense from, then they will have that
fundamental limitation. So if they have the ability to
perceive how someone is feeling, then they might be able
to reason on top of that. But if they are fundamentally--
the interface is primitive, and you are just talking to them
by textual means or by saying, here's the sequence,
then, of course, they will have
fundamental limits on what you can do with
that limited information. ERIC TOPOL: Well, I think
that's a good stopping point in that we're still

[APPLAUSE] JAMES MANYIKA: For
our first session, I'm honored to introduce the
pathbreaking scientist, Dr. Jennifer Doudna. Jennifer is a foreign
member of the Royal Society. She won the Nobel Prize
in chemistry in 2020 for her co-discovery of CRISPR. She is the president and founder
of the Innovative Genomics Institute and a co-founder and
advisor to several companies developing unique CRISPR-based
solutions to tackle some of the greatest
health challenges-- the greatest challenges in
health, agriculture and climate. She is considered
one of the foremost and most pioneering influential
scientists of our time. Please help me in welcoming
Jennifer to the stage. [APPLAUSE] Welcome. JENNIFER DOUDNA: James,
great to be here. JAMES MANYIKA: Good to have you. Now, Jennifer, it's been,
what, four years since you won the Nobel
Prize in chemistry. And since then, a
lot has happened. I'm curious, would
you share with us what's top of mind for you, both
in your research but also things going on in your field? JENNIFER DOUDNA: Well,
it's amazing to be here. What an incredible opportunity
to visit with other scientists and hear perspectives on
AI and science, something we think about a lot
in my own research and at the Innovative
Genomics Institute. I think with CRISPR, which is
a technology for editing DNA precisely in cells
and organisms, what we're seeing is
a real step change in the pace at which this
technology is being applied. And one great example
of this is the approval of the first CRISPR
therapeutic to treat patients with sickle cell disease. It was actually approved here
in the UK first about a year ago and, shortly after
that, in the US. And it's a functional cure
for a human genetic disease. It means that patients
with this terrible disease can receive a one-and-done
treatment that deals with their disease
at the genetic level. It makes a change to DNA that
overrides the sickle-cell mutation and means that these
patients are, at least so far, have been symptom free-- JAMES MANYIKA: Oh, wow. JENNIFER DOUDNA: --since
receiving the therapy. It's extraordinary. Several dozen people
have now been treated. And so it's-- for
everybody in the field, it's been a tremendously
exciting moment and really illustrates the partnership
between scientists, of course, patients that were voluntary
participants in clinical trials, and then, of course, the
companies that have been making all of this possible. So I think it's a reminder of
why we do the science that we do and the extraordinary
opportunities that lie ahead. JAMES MANYIKA: No,
it's incredible to see how you go from the
science breakthroughs to actual things that start to
make a difference and drugs that start to make a
difference for people. Now, obviously, how AI
fits into this picture is top of mind for many of us. What do you see as the key
opportunity areas for AI within gene editing and CRISPR,
but also just in genomics research more generally? JENNIFER DOUDNA: Well, one
of the things with CRISPR in thinking about this
approval, this drug approval, is how we expand
the applicability to people around the world. Right now, this therapy costs
about $2 million US per patient. So it's very expensive,
meaning that it's difficult to get a hold of
financially for many people. But also, just the
way it's applied, it requires a
bone-marrow transplant to receive this therapy. So we see a future where
we override that barrier by discovering better ways
to deliver CRISPR enzymes, making CRISPR enzymes that
are more efficient at what they do, so you need to use
fewer molecules to achieve a clinical benefit,
and then, of course, expanding the utility of
this gene-editing platform in other areas. And all of those are spaces
where AI will play a big role, I feel. And also, it should be
mentioned that CRISPR, in addition to
being a tool that is directly applicable clinically
and also in the environment-- I'm sure we'll talk about that-- it's also an extraordinary
research tool. So it generates a
lot of data that are then suitable for mining
by AI-mediated approaches. JAMES MANYIKA: So
does that, in the end, also accelerate the
research pipeline? Does it mean that we'll get
drug therapies and other things faster? JENNIFER DOUDNA: I
think, no question. That's not happened yet. But I think many of us see
that that's on the horizon. JAMES MANYIKA: Yeah. You know, when most of us
think about gene editing, we tend to think of medical
therapies as the arena where this will play
out and benefit. But it's actually much
broader, isn't it? I mean, at your
institute, you're starting to work on
other areas, like climate change and other things. Say more about that. What do you see as the broad
applications of gene editing? JENNIFER DOUDNA: Well, when we
founded the Innovative Genomics Institute about 10
years ago, our thinking was to focus in both
health care and climate. These are two areas where
we think gene editing is going to have a major impact. And people don't
necessarily think about genome editing
and CRISPR when they think about climate change. But we see incredible
opportunities both to make changes
in plants that do things like allow better
carbon storage in the soil through root systems, and to
create drought-resistant plants like rice, which is
something we're doing at the Innovative Genomics
Institute, and also, frankly, to use
CRISPR at its source, namely in the
microbes that populate our environment, and our bodies,
and that impact our health and, of course,
commercial agriculture. JAMES MANYIKA:
Well, you're going to have to tell us about the
cow that Hannah mentioned. JENNIFER DOUDNA: Oh, yeah. The cow, right? Yeah. So we were talking
at dinner last night about where many of
us see the immediate, or, say, in the next five
years, impact of AI and science. And my answer was
a methane-free cow. It turns out that cattle are one
of the major sources of methane, which is a powerful
greenhouse gas that's emitted through agricultural
practices around the world. Imagine that we
could create cows that were literally not burping
out methane and, therefore, using their feed
more productively. Farmers would like this and,
of course, the rest of us would like it. And so this is
something that we're working on at the
Innovative Genomics Institute in partnership with
University of California, Davis scientists who have
demonstrated that, in principle, this is absolutely possible. They do it today with
complicated manipulations of the cow diet. We see a future where
genome editing could be used in the cow microbiome,
which are the microbes that populate the cow rumen, which
are actually producing methane, and change their metabolism so
that no longer is a problem. JAMES MANYIKA: Oh, wow. Oh, wow. Well, it's extraordinary
to hear you talk about this and how gene editing has kind of
gone from the stuff of science fiction to actually being
kind of an established tool and protocol for how we
do a lot of other things, and also how you
and your colleagues are navigating
through all of this. Well, here, perhaps, is a
provocative question, if I may. Do you think advances in AI make
certain elements of gene editing obsolete? JENNIFER DOUDNA:
I actually think that there's an
incredible opportunity for a collaborative approach. I think that AI advances our
capabilities as scientists, or it can. And what I see is a future where
we can more accurately predict the outcomes of
experiments, for example, genome-editing experiments. We know which genes
need editing and which ones are superfluous to
an outcome that's desired, even predicting things
like the impact of editing over time in an organism. And, I think, that will actually
accelerate the pace of discovery even faster than it
is now, and, we hope, make some experimentation
unnecessary. So, I think, that would actually
be an incredible advance if that comes to pass. JAMES MANYIKA: So it might
even help steer, suggest, or guide what experiments to
do, and which ones not to do, and which ones to
explore, and so forth. That's extraordinary. JENNIFER DOUDNA: Exactly. JAMES MANYIKA: One of the
things you've made observations about with CRISPR,
and we're also starting to see in AI, is
the resource-intensive nature of the research itself,
which is also shifting traditional relationships
between academia and the private sector,
and also changing how we think about funding. How do you assess the
current funding landscape? And what can we do to change, to
facilitate the kind of research like CRISPR or AlphaFold
level breakthroughs happen even faster and, perhaps,
even more collaboratively? JENNIFER DOUDNA:
Well, James, I've been an academic
for a long time. I've been running an
academic lab 30 years. And I'm also now more recently
a founder of companies and have worked a lot with
new company teams that are building groups
that are focused on particular
applications of CRISPR. And what I see is
that, I think, there's an incredible opportunity that
hasn't quite been advanced yet and in which we, Innovative
Genomics Institute, would like to be at
the forefront of, which is to think
about how we fund innovative early-stage science
to enable would-be entrepreneurs to make the kinds of
creative discoveries that will then be the
foundations of new companies. And frankly, it can't,
at least in the US-- I don't know how it
is here in the UK, but I imagine you face
similar challenges. It can be very difficult
to get government funding for very early stage, high-risk,
but potentially very high-payoff ideas, especially with young
scientists who are just starting their careers. And that's really what we aim
to do at Innovative Genomics Institute, is to fund
those scientists. And we do it in a
way that enables them to work collaboratively,
which is often not necessarily rewarded in academic settings. We actually reward that. We ask them to work
together in teams, and we ask them to consider
interdisciplinary approaches to problems. And so, for example, our
microbiome work with the cow is very much that way, where we
have computer scientists, even electrical engineers, and
agricultural scientists who are all working together. These are normally, at
least in academics, not people who would necessarily
bump into each other. But we build the teams
that make them possible. JAMES MANYIKA:
No, I have to say, it's really quite inspiring,
Jennifer, to hear you. You continue to do this
extraordinary, pathbreaking scientific work but
also sounds like you're starting to innovate how we do
science, and create ventures, and structures in
order to do science. I have to ask you this,
though, which is-- you have a unique point of view. As somebody who has been in the
eye of the storm, if you like, with regards to
research breakthrough that people, on the one
hand, find hugely exciting and, sometimes, also
find quite concerning with societal implications,
what can all of us working on AI learn from your
experience with CRISPR? Because you could
argue AI has some of the same exciting,
and complicated, and concerning characteristics. JENNIFER DOUDNA: Absolutely. Well, I think back to 2000-- I think it was 2014,
the end of 2014. We had published our
work on CRISPR in 2012. And by the end of
2014, scientists were already using
CRISPR in monkey embryos that were implanted
and were genome-edited, not just in particular tissues
but in the entire organism, such that they could pass those gene
edits to future generations. And so when I read
that academic paper, it was very clear
that this would-- I didn't see any reason why it
wouldn't be possible to do it in human beings. And as some of you here
may know, that, in fact, did come to pass. And so what that motivated for
me was the realization that I couldn't-- it wasn't going to be
enough just to put my head down and work in the lab or
work with our institute. We really had to be
implementing a whole team-- and we have this now at IGI-- that works on public impact. And we ended up involving the
Royal Society, and the National Academies in the United
States, as well as equivalent organizations in
other countries, to boot up a series of
international meetings on human-genome editing. And so, in fact, the
most recent of those was held here in London
in March of last year. And that's led to a very open,
transparent conversation. It's gotten groups involved,
including the World Health Organization, to encourage a
transparent database of work that's going on using genome
editing by scientists, especially if they're using
it in the human germline or for applications in the
environment that could also have global impact and could be
dangerous if not used properly. So, I think, one
thing that we've learned from that experience is
that it's incredibly important to encourage that kind of
open, transparent conversation. It can't just
involve scientists. It has to include
other stakeholders. We work very hard at Innovative
Genomics Institute to make knowledge and data accessible
to people that are not experts. So we have a lot of high-school
teachers, for example, that we work with, that-- we give them resources to
help educate their students about genome editing, what it
means, and where it's going. So, I think, this
is something that will be incredibly valuable
also in the AI space, doing the same kind
of encouragement of public discussion,
knowledge-based decision making. I mean, this is just so
important, especially now. JAMES MANYIKA: Absolutely. And we're trying to learn from
your examples and the paths and approaches you've taken. I mean, in much of the work that
we do, including in AlphaFold. I mean, my colleagues
Demis, and John, and others went through
an extraordinary process with ethicists and others
before that work ever saw the light of day
to think through, what are the implications and
possibilities of all of that? But there's so much
more still to learn. But I wanted to just to thank
you for taking the time. There's so much to be
excited about here, and it's an absolute
honor to have been in conversation with you. And thank you for all
the work you're doing. JENNIFER DOUDNA: Thank you. Thank you for
including me, James. Great to be here. [APPLAUSE]

=== VIDEO: YaRWtrnG6TA (https://youtu.be/YaRWtrnG6TA) ===


[APPLAUSE] JAMES MANYIKA: AI's
impact on science has been in the
headlines lately, but the potential for
AI to advance science has long been a motivating force
for many of us in the field, dating back to the early AI
researchers, such as Alan Turing and Christopher
Longuet-Higgins, and to many in recent
decades, including my colleagues at Google
DeepMind and Google Research. The excitement
around AI in science is not because of
a belief that AI is a replacement for
scientists, but because many of the confounding
problems in science benefit from the use of
computational techniques, thus making AI a
powerful tool to assist the work of scientists. We saw early signs of
this assistive capability with the potential in
the work of Hodgkins and Huxley's use of
computational approaches to describe how
nerve impulses travel along neurons, work
that would win them the Nobel Prize in 1963. Fast forward to my colleagues,
Demis Hassabis, John Jumper, and the AlphaFold Team,
whose work using AI recently won the Nobel
Prize in chemistry, solving the protein
folding problem, a remarkable achievement. This is a problem that was posed
by Nobel laureate Christian Anfinsen in the 1970s. So how is AI helping
advance science? I'll start with speed. In some areas of science,
increasingly capable AI is making it possible for us
to condense hundreds or even thousands of years of research
into a few years, months, or even days. AI's also helping expand the
scope of research, enabling scientists to look
at many things at once and in many new
ways, rather than one by one. In addition, AI advances, along
with the access to the insights from using it, are
enabling many more people to participate in research
so that we can further accelerate scientific discovery
to the benefit of society. Let me share briefly
a few examples of how AI is enabling
landmark advances, starting with the work of
Demis and John with AlphaFold. With AlphaFold, over
the course of a year, my colleagues were
able to predict the structure of nearly every
protein known to science, over 200 million of them. And with AlphaFold 3, they've
extended beyond proteins to all of life's biomolecules
and their interactions, including DNA, RNA, and ligands. To date, AlphaFold has
been used by more than 2 million researchers in
more than 190 countries, working on problems ranging
from neglected diseases to drug resistant bacteria. AlphaSense, which
builds on AlphaFold, enabled my colleagues
to categorize almost 89% of the 71 possible
missense variants. These are single letter
substitutions in DNA, and they were able
to categorize them as likely pathogenic or benign. By contrast, only 0.1% had been
confirmed by human experts, albeit in more detail. When the human
genome was initially sequenced, an
incredible achievement, it was based on a
single genomic assembly. Last year, my colleagues
in Google Research, using AI tools and
work with a consortium of academic collaborators,
released the first draft reference human pangenome. This was based on 47
genomic assemblies, thus better representing
human genetic diversity. In neuroscience, a
10 year collaboration between my colleagues in
Google Research, the Max Planck Institute, and the
Lichtman Lab at Harvard recently produced
a nanoscale mapping of a piece of the human brain. That is at a level of detail
never previously achieved before. This project
revealed never before seen structures in
the human brain that may change our understanding
of how the brain works and maybe perhaps open pathways
to understanding diseases like Alzheimer's and many more. The full mapping of this has
been made publicly available for researchers to build on. Beyond the life
sciences, we're seeing progress in other domains. In a landmark achievement
for climate modeling, we combine machine learning
with traditional physics-based approach to build NeuralGCM. This allows us to simulate
the atmosphere more accurately and efficiently. In fact, NeuralGCM can
simulate over 70,000 days of the atmosphere in the time
it would take a state of the art physics-based model to
simulate only 19 days. There are other
similar breakthroughs, such as the work by my
colleagues at Google DeepMind with GraphCast, which is able
to provide very accurate 10 day forecasts that are much
better than the gold standard in weather forecasting. Our quantum AI team
is making progress on questions that were
previously the realm of science fiction, like studying
the characteristics of traversable wormholes. This opens up new possibilities
for testing quantum gravity theories, originally posed
with the Einstein-Rosen bridge almost 90 years ago. In fact, quantum
is an area where we're beginning to see promising
bi-directional reinforcement between AI and science. In one direction, AI is
advancing our progress in quantum computing. And in the other, quantum is
helping advance research in AI. There are many
other such examples that we're working on in
material science, fusion, mathematics, and more,
and in all of these, in deep collaboration
with academic scientists. Beyond such
breakthroughs in science, AI is also advancing science
in ways that are already providing tangible benefits
for real people in areas like climate change
and health care. Let me start with an example
from climate adaptation. Flood forecasting is a much
more frequent and urgent problem due to climate change. And now advances
in AI have enabled us to fill large gaps in data
to predict riverine floods, up to seven days in advance with
the same accuracy as now casts. After an initial
pilot in Bangladesh, our early warning
platform, Flood Hub, now covers 100 countries,
covering 700 million people with such flood forecasts. And in an example in
climate mitigation, consider the following. The formation of contrails,
those streaks of white that you see behind
airplanes, has long been known as a driver
of emissions in aviation, accounting for as much as 35%
of aviation's global warming impact. My colleagues in Google
Research developed an AI model that predicts where
contrails are likely to form and, in partnership
with American Airlines, tested this in over 70 flights. We measured the impact and found
a 54% reduction in emissions. Similarly, AI offers much
promise for disease detection, especially in low resource
communities and countries and regions of the world. For example, eight years
ago, Google researchers found that AI could accurately
interpret retinal scans to detect diabetic retinopathy,
a preventable cause of blindness that affects roughly
100 million people. We developed a screening tool
that's been used in more than 600,000 screenings worldwide. And in new partnerships
in Thailand and India will enable 6 million screenings
over the coming few years. We've been implementing
other examples, including in tuberculosis,
colorectal cancer, breast cancer, and maternal health. Despite all this progress,
this is just the beginning. There's still so
much more to do. I see three areas of focus
to help to fully realize AI's potential to
help advance science and bring tangible
benefits to society. First, we need to
continue to make progress on AI's current limitations
and shortcomings and to increase
AI's capabilities to be able to
assist in developing novel scientific concepts,
theories, experiments and more. Second, we need a
sustained commitment to the scientific method,
something my colleague Demis Hassabis is always
talking about, imploring us to
pay attention to, and to also focus on
responsible approaches to using AI to advance science. We need scientists,
ethicists, and safety experts, like many in this
room, working together to address the risks most
particular to science, like viruses and bioweapons,
as well as challenges, like bias in data sets,
privacy preservation, and environmental impacts. Third, we need to prioritize
making AI-enabled research tools and resources more accessible to
more scientists in more places and to make sure the progress we
make from all of this benefits people everywhere. I'm very excited
about what lies ahead in all of this in this
new era of discovery. There is so much
we can do together to build tools that help advance
science and benefit everyone. And there's so much we can do
to enable amazing scientists, including all of you in
this room and elsewhere, in their work. We'll be hearing from some of
these extraordinary scientists today.
