HANNAH FRY: Welcome back to
"Google DeepMind: The Podcast" with me, your host,
Professor Hannah Fry. Now, those of you who've been
following us from the beginning, you will know that using
artificial intelligence to enhance a scientific
understanding of the world has always been a key
goal of the team here. You've got weather forecasting,
that's advanced meteorology. You've got nuclear
fusion for physicists, and perhaps most
notably is AlphaFold, which has been an absolute
game changer for biology. But if all of that seems
a little bit academic, a bit niche, perhaps, the kind
of stuff that should only really be exciting to a handful of
scientists in their labs, well, then hopefully, today's
episode will persuade you of the substantial impact
that these ideas can have on humanity. Because I am joined, once again,
by the guy who is in charge of scientific research
here at "Google DeepMind," Pushmeet Kohli. Pushmeet, thank you so much for
joining us back on the podcast. Quite a lot has happened
since we last spoke to you. How many nature papers
have you got now? PUSHMEET KOHLI: I'm
not really sure. We're not counting. HANNAH FRY: Too many to count. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: So I want to talk to
you about AlphaFold because it feels as though this is one of
the most exciting things that has advanced even further
since I last spoke to you. And I should say,
actually, that anybody who wants to know more
about how AlphaFold works and what it does, can
go back to episode one of the previous
series for more detail, but I guess just briefly, can
you summarize what AlphaFold is? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. So AlphaFold is a system
that, given a protein-- proteins make
everything around us. They are the building
blocks of life. And essentially, they
can be represented as a sequence of amino acids. And it's a great
mystery as to what is the structure of
any given protein. So if you are given a sequence
of these amino acids, a protein, what is its structure? And that's really important
because that informs the function of that protein. What AlphaFold does, it
takes a sequence of a protein and predicts its structure. And that's really
important for scientists to understand what is the
function of that protein. So that's what AlphaFold did. It solved that 50-year-old grand
challenge in science by making scientists able to find the
structure of any given protein in a matter of seconds. HANNAH FRY: So I guess actually,
a more accurate statement would be it was a
mystery but is no longer. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. There's still a few things that
are unknown about how proteins behave, the dynamics
of the proteins, and so on, but the one
standard big problem in protein understanding, What is the
structure of a protein? is now-- we now know about it. HANNAH FRY: So what's
changed since last time then, since the last series
when there was AlphaFold 2? What can AlphaFold3 do that
the previous versions couldn't? PUSHMEET KOHLI: That's
a very good question. Now, I said proteins are
the building blocks of life, but they are not the only
molecules in our body. Our body has many
other biomolecules. We have nucleic
acids, RNA, DNA, which are the recipe of life, what
makes you and me, Hannah. Then there are small
molecules, drugs that interact with these proteins. There are antibodies. There are so many
different types of molecules that are
interacting in any living being. And we want to
understand the structure of all these molecules. And that's what
AlphaFold 3 unlocks. It not only gives you the
structure of a protein, but it also gives
you the structure of, how do these proteins
form complexes or they connect to each other? Or how do they interact with
small molecules or antibodies and so on? HANNAH FRY: What's been the
reaction then from scientists now that this has had
a bit of time to be out there in the world? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So it was
very hard to actually-- my background, I'm a computer
scientist by training, and it was very hard for
me to actually understand the impact of AlphaFold. And the first time I realized, I
went to a conference, a biology conference, where a biologist
spoke to me and said, well, there was this protein
that I've been trying to study for the last 10 years. And I have collected
so much data about it, but still the structure of
that protein was a mystery. HANNAH FRY: Because it was
so, so, so hard to work out what the structure
might be before AlphaFold. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. And the scientists
had used AlphaFold and had the structure,
and then the question he had was, well,
what do I do now? I have the structure, and so-- like he had to completely
rethink what biology requires now in terms of the next steps. HANNAH FRY: A 10-year
project, and he just put it into AlphaFold, and it sorted
it out for him in a few minutes or maybe overnight. Was he excited or was that in
some ways a bit demoralizing? PUSHMEET KOHLI: No. I think there was a
mixture of both surprise but also excitement, something
that you wouldn't imagine. It's like, imagine giving people
an ability that did not exist. Imagine the first time
telephones came about, and you could now talk to
people who are miles away. This was unimaginable. And the fact that you
can take any protein, you can put in the
sequence of that protein, and visualize what
the 3D structure is, that just gave the scientists
a superpower that they had not imagined earlier. And there was a
lot of excitement as to what we could do
with that superpower. HANNAH FRY: And is that how
people are feeling, like they've got a superpower now? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. So one of the things that we
have seen is AlphaFold database. So we created this
AlphaFold database, which is, we found
the structures of almost all known proteins. And we put the structures
for those in a database hosted by the European
microbiology lab, our partners, EMBL-EBI, and this was
available-- these 250 million structures were available to
anyone in the world for free. And that particular database has
been used across 140 countries by 1.8 million scientists. And the fact that there are
1.8 million scientists who are looking for
protein structures, if that is not a
positive statement about the state of the world,
then I don't know what is. We think about
the doom and gloom in society, but if you look at
how science has progressed-- there are 1.8 million people
who are studying proteins. HANNAH FRY: So I think the
really notable thing when you talk to people
about AlphaFold is just how big of
a difference there is between the people
who understand it, that 1.8 million people,
and everybody else. Is this something that
you notice as well? AlphaFold doesn't-- it feels
a bit technical to really understand the magnitude of it. What are the applications
that you are excited about that other
people will understand? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I
think, for a large section of the community, they see
AlphaFold as an AI breakthrough in the sciences,
but the scientists who work on this topic
understand the implications of it in a very deep way. They know the
implications of AlphaFold for extremely important
things like drug discovery, designing a new
vaccine, thinking about antimicrobial resistance,
thinking about new enzymes to decompose plastics. I can just go on and on and on. HANNAH FRY: But that's
not the only project that has been going on in here
since we last spoke to you? So tell us a little
bit more about what you've been working on. PUSHMEET KOHLI: So
we have been working on a whole spectrum
of different topics, from material science
to fusion, to working on new discoveries
in computer science, in mathematics, weather
prediction, meteorology. So there's a whole spectrum of
areas that we are looking at. HANNAH FRY: And of
those, where do you think there's been really
significant progress in the last couple of years? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So one of the
moments that we can talk about is our work on
weather prediction. Weather and climate is
something that we are all thinking about at the
moment, but if you look at what DeepMind
had done earlier, we had our nowcasting
model, which was able to make very good
prediction but at very short timescales. With our new model
called GraphCast, which we released
last year, we can now look at the problem
of 10 day forecasts. And what we have shown that this
new model can make these 10 day predictions more accurately
than some of the models that are being used, the
classical models that are being used by
the Met Office. HANNAH FRY: The supercomputers. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. Which run on supercomputers
for many hours, and we can outperform
them in terms of accuracy and make predictions in a matter
of a minute on a single chip. I think in some
sense, this really opens up research in this area. So a lot of other people,
a lot of other entities can now conduct research
in weather prediction. And the results are amazing. One particular example
that was fascinating is that there was this cyclone,
Hurricane Lee, last year, which made landfall in Nova Scotia. And our model, GraphCast,
was able to make the prediction of the landfall
event nine days earlier-- HANNAH FRY: Wow. PUSHMEET KOHLI: --while the
classical models were only able to do it six days earlier. So they could give a three
day additional heads up. HANNAH FRY: It's a
superpower that doesn't just apply to biology, then. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Absolutely. The amount of impact that
AI and machine learning is having across all these
disciplines is amazing. And if you think about
it, it feels natural because, in any of
these areas of science, we are collecting a lot of data,
and the complexity of models that we are playing with
and we are working with is really expanding. And it's just very natural
that a single human mind has difficulty in
comprehending what are the real patterns in this data. And machine learning
and AI just give you that ability to figure
out what is needed in many of these problems. HANNAH FRY: You mentioned
material science there. Frame for us the general
problem, as it were, in material science pre AI? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So what is the
problem of material discovery? You want to discover materials
which have certain properties. We have gone through all
these ages, from the Stone Age to the Iron Age to the
Bronze Age, and so on. And at every age, we are
working with a new material, and new material gives
us new abilities. And the problem of
material discovery is to discover materials which
have certain useful properties. Now, how was that done till now? It was done in a very
experimental sort of way. People try different
things in the lab. Sometimes the things
worked out as expected. Sometimes, things didn't
work out as expected. But there was no-- there were some rules of thumb. There were some
theory, but we did not know the extent to what
was possible in materials. HANNAH FRY: Often things
get discovered by accident, like vulcanized rubber or-- PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: Just sort of a
chance happening in a lab, and then a new material that
turns out to be really useful pops up. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. Like graphene. We all know about
this story about how this magical material was
isolated by sellotape, by taking pieces of carbon and then
repeatedly making it thinner and thinner by a sticky tape. And it has amazing properties. HANNAH FRY: Ending up being
the thinnest substance that can be manufactured. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: A single atom thick. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. And it has a very
interesting properties in terms of
conductivity and so on. So before AI and before
even computational methods, that essentially was the norm. That people-- and even
today, in some sense, material science is a
very experimental science, where people are trying
to discover new materials for whether it's for
constructing a battery or for a photovoltaic cell or
for a superconductor and so on. Now, how computational
systems are used today is to then post-hoc
rationalize, why is that material behaving
in a way that it's behaving? But we are very
far from the place where we could rationally
design a material. Given a property, you
say, find me the material, invent a new material which
can maximize these properties. And that's what the
problem is for AI. Can we somehow in silico
de novo from scratch start to invent any given
material with some property? HANNAH FRY: So that
you can go in and say, I want something that is
extraordinarily flexible, extraordinarily light, whatever,
easy to mine, something, something, something. And then it's just like, here's
the physical structure of the-- atomic structure that will
result in that material. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. That's the vision. HANNAH FRY: Let's anchor
this to an example, then. So you mentioned
batteries there. We've got batteries. What's wrong with the
batteries we have? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I
think they're doing well. They're lithium ion batteries. There are a number
of issues with that. First, they are based on certain
resources which are difficult. For example, the lithium
ion batteries, the batteries used today use cobalt,
which is difficult to mine. We might want to
increase the energy density of these
batteries in the future. We might want to make them
more thermally stable. We might want to
make them cheaper. And if you could somehow have
a magic sort of material, which can have higher energy
density, is more stable, is easy to manufacture,
then, of course, you would want to
transition to it. HANNAH FRY: And find it without
just waiting for an accident to happen in a lab. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: Are you sure
there is one, though? How do you know that
lithium isn't just the best that the universe has to offer? PUSHMEET KOHLI: It could
be that lithium is the best and we were extremely
lucky, and we found it, but that seems very remote. There is, the number
of materials-- just to give you an example,
there were around 20,000 inorganic materials
that people play with. Now, using
computational methods, 28,000 have been found out. So they are roughly 40,000,
50,000 known materials that were known to be stable,
in the sense that, at 0 Kelvin or under some
theoretical situations, they will not decompose
to other materials. HANNAH FRY: So if
you freeze them right down to absolute zero,
they don't split apart. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. So they are stable materials. So that was what was known in
the material science community. Our new AI model known
last year expanded that set and said there are 2.2
million new inorganic materials that are stable. HANNAH FRY: From 50,000? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: Wow. Wow. That's massive. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Right. And you're talking
about graphene, there are 52,000 single chain
layered materials in that set. So the number of possibilities,
the number of things that now we can search over is immense. And you asked the
question whether the lithium-cobalt batteries
are the optimal batteries, well, there are so many things in that
2.2 million set that one of them could be much, much better. HANNAH FRY: The chances that
lithium is actually quite-- what's it called? GNNME? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: What
does it stand for? PUSHMEET KOHLI:
Graph neural networks for material exploration. HANNAH FRY: And
how does it work? How are you deciding new
material structures that haven't yet been discovered? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So
it essentially tries to start with existing
structures and compositions. HANNAH FRY: 50,000 that we know. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. And says, OK, let me
change some of those, and then, I'll learn
a model to say which of those changes, which of
those modified materials are stable or not stable. And it is able to do those
calculations very efficiently, and that's where the machine
learning model comes in. It's able to make predictions
about the stability and the free energy of
these new compositions and these new crystal structures
in a much more accurate way. HANNAH FRY: So in
one sense, then, taking the 50,000 and then doing
some kind of atomic shuffling, if you like, just trying
different combinations, but then, the clever bit,
as you're describing, is that you can calculate
without actually having made ever this sort of fantasy
material which has been constructed by atomic shuffling. You can tell whether or not
it will be stable at 0 degree Kelvin if you made it. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: How? PUSHMEET KOHLI: You can
do that theoretically, but you will need to do a lot
of very complex calculations. And what this
model is able to do is basically approximate
those calculations and do it much, much
computationally inexpensively. HANNAH FRY: So in
many ways, actually, I can see the similarities
between this and AlphaFold. You're talking about the
atomic structure of something, right down at the level, I
mean amino acids and atoms, and then predicting larger
structural properties as a result. PUSHMEET KOHLI: So in
this particular case, you are given a new crystal
structure or a new composition, and you are told, is it
going to be stable or not? And the model is
able to say that. HANNAH FRY: How do you
validate it, though? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So we have
validated these in two ways. One form of validation
is by doing calculations. So when I said, these are 2.2
million stable structures, what do I mean? How do I say that
these are stable? There are theories
in quantum chemistry which give us approaches to
figure out whether something is going to be stable. These are very
difficult calculations. So we can run those
calculations on the predictions that GNNME has made and
see whether the theory says that those will be stable. So that's one way. And then we have also taken
a subset of the most stable predictions and then
experimentally try to validate them in the lab. HANNAH FRY: Like build it? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: Oh, wow. But then, hold on. If it's giving you some
fantasy atomic structure, does it tell you how to make it? PUSHMEET KOHLI: No, it doesn't. So then we have to figure
out how do you make it. HANNAH FRY: And you
have managed to do this with a small number of them? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: Right. And do they hold up? Are they stable? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. Yeah. And large proportion
of them are stable. HANNAH FRY: Give me numbers. Give me numbers. PUSHMEET KOHLI: This is
constantly changing-- HANNAH FRY: Of course. PUSHMEET KOHLI: --but the
success rates are quite high. Between 70% to 90% of those
that we try in the lab end up being stable. And then now once we have
those valid materials, then we also have
to think about, what are the properties
of those materials? Now, which of them would
be better batteries or would be useful
in photovoltaics or would be good in
superconductivity, and so on? HANNAH FRY: So can it also
predict the properties, then? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So no, not
our current generation. It just makes the predictions
about the stability of those structures,
but we are working on new models for these
other types of problems. HANNAH FRY: What kind of
materials are you hoping for? Battery materials
is one example. What other kind
of things are you hoping that GNNME one
will help develop? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So
one of the key things that people have
been thinking about is materials that can
exhibit superconductivity. HANNAH FRY: By which we mean? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Which
means, basically, they exhibit zero resistance. And why is that important? That's important because,
if you have such a material, you can create very
strong magnetic fields. You can store a lot of
energy using those materials. Why are magnetic
fields important? They're important
from everything to do with MRI scanners to
creating fusion reactors. The magnets that are
used in fusion reactors require very high
magnetic fields. So superconductivity is an
extremely important property. HANNAH FRY: And
at the moment, you have to super cool
things in order to be able to get up
to those high levels? Because otherwise,
it just gets too hot. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. And so what is the Holy
Grail in material science is to discover a room
temperature superconductor. And there have been
many attempts at it and some false starts, but-- HANNAH FRY: And some
quite cheeky pretending. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: This happen too. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah, that too. But I think one day,
if we discover it, it will be transformational. HANNAH FRY: And do
you think AI will be involved in that discovery? PUSHMEET KOHLI:
Yeah, I'm absolutely sure that AI will aid in the
search for superconductors, definitely. HANNAH FRY: At the very
least, accelerate the process. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: So that part
of making it, though. the way that you described
it was like, yeah, we've got to figure
out how to make it. So it feels like we're skipping
over quite a bit tricky part of the process. Is there any way that you
can automate that bit too? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. Once you know what the material
is, then it's a whole-- there is another sort of stream
of material science work, which is, how do you make
something cheaply? And there is a lot of work
happening in that area as well, but at the moment, our
team is looking more on the discovery side. And just to say, we
have to be also be cautious in saying
that a lot of it-- yes, there are these 2.2 million
materials, but how many of them, as you said, are
valid materials, which actually will prove
out when synthesized? How many of them are useful and
will have interesting material properties? All of that are open questions. But I think the fact that
there is this new place that we find ourselves in in that-- from 20,000 to 2.2 million,
it's a different ball game. HANNAH FRY: Absolutely. Here's another open question. How long do you think it will be
before the first AI developed-- or material developed
with the assistance of AI makes a big difference? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I think,
unlike biology, materials science is much more
experimental at the moment. Drug discovery is still-- and biology still has used
computational methods, but material science is
much more experimental. So it will take time, but I
think in the next 5 to 10 years, we will see really, as I say,
in silico material design starting to make a big impact. HANNAH FRY: So I just want to
zoom out a little bit further, because-- so you're sitting
here talking to us very confidently about material
science but also about biology. And you're a computer scientist. So how come you got into this? PUSHMEET KOHLI: The last time I
studied science was in school, and I came to DeepMind
hoping to work on general intelligent systems
and make them safe and reliable. But I was always
fascinated about, how can intelligent systems
have real-world impact? And that's what I used to keep
on having discussions with Demis with. And one day he
comes to my office and says, oh, Pushmeet, I think
we have a good role for you. And I said, OK, what the role? And I was assuming
some product area that I'm going to finally
use machine learning for. And he said, we want to
start a science team, and you should lead it. And I said, why me? Science, I have no background. I studied science in school. I loved it at school, but then
I became a computer scientist, and then that's what I did. But he said, well, see you're
into multidisciplinary research. You want to understand
the problems and you want to have
real-world impact. And what better
place to have impact in pushing the
boundaries of knowledge forward in the natural sciences? So I said, OK. I agreed. And then over the
next couple of months, I found what I've
got myself into, because, if somebody
was designing a job to experience imposter
syndrome, this is the job. Because you will be working
with Nobel Prize winners and trying to understand
those problems, and you don't have no clue. And you're trying to
make a difference, and you start from Wikipedia. HANNAH FRY: You
start from Wikipedia? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. You start from Wikipedia
and try to get a crash course in the area, but-- I mean the scientific
community is amazing. People are there who are
willing to sit down with you, are willing to teach you or
go on that journey with you. HANNAH FRY: I guess the
really key point, though, is that you really,
really, really understand the heart of these algorithms. You really understand
the type of systems that they can perform well in
and where they can really excel. And so in some ways,
the fact that you're talking about lots
of different areas, meteorology on the one hand,
material science on another, then biology,
actually, I can see how there are these,
behind the scenes, these actual technical
similarities between the systems that you're designing. So in many ways,
being a generalist, it requires a generalist to be
able to do this kind of stuff? PUSHMEET KOHLI:
Yeah, absolutely. If you think about intelligence,
what is intelligence? How do you create intelligence? Intelligence does not
happen in a vacuum. Suddenly, you have a system
which gets the intuitions and is able to make
these new discoveries. It happens through experience. And what form of experience? How rich is your experience? And this holds true
for humans as well. The amount of things
that we see in our life, it gives us a
different perspective. And so, with these models,
the richness of the data that they see, whether
that data is collected by a scientist in a lab or
whether that data is generated by simulations, if
it is rich enough-- you want the data
to be rich enough so that the model is forced
to learn general concepts to explain that data. And then the other
sort of element is to have good
evaluation metrics. Machine learning methods
are very good at cheating. They are amazing
at memorization. They are like the student
that can memorize anything. So if you just give them a lot
of data, they will memorize it. And you ask them any question
from the-- which is close to the training set, and
they will say, ah, yeah, this is the answer, but
they actually don't-- HANNAH FRY: Understand
what's going on. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: I guess
that must have happened quite a
lot with proteins at some point or another. They've just seen they've peaked
at the back of the textbook. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. And so what we had
to do is make sure that we made that
test extremely hard, made it impossible to cheat. And the true test of a good
machine learning system is its ability to generalize, to
unseen examples, to have those-- to make those
predictions in things which it had never seen before. So understanding what
is the right data and what is the right
evaluation metric for a different
scientific discipline is one part of the problem. It was one of the things that I
needed to work with our teams. And then the other
part of the solution is, in the design
of these machine learning models and
these AI models, you want to learn from
data, but you also want to bake in any
information that you can about the problem into the
design of the model itself. And that is where domain
knowledge makes a lot of sense. You don't want to start from
scratch if you don't have to. So in all our
projects, we started with a very
multidisciplinary viewpoint. We got in experts, we asked
them what was working, what was not working, and
tried to inject everything that was currently known into
the design of the model itself. HANNAH FRY: But it's noticeable
that the common theme of everything that
you've described there is where there is a
version of success. There's a right answer
that you are looking for, like the true underlying
structure of the protein, whether it's truly
stable or not, whether you really are
predicting the weather. Is that absolutely crucial,
then, for any of these projects? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Absolutely. You really need to
have a good sense of what does success look like. HANNAH FRY: So then when it
comes to choosing projects-- because, I mean, if you are,
as you say, the generalist who can, with domain-specific
knowledge, adapt these algorithms
to anything, how do you decide
what's worth your time? PUSHMEET KOHLI: That's
a very good question. And there are a number of
dimensions that we look at. One dimension is the data. If there is no data and
there is no experience, the machine learning model
will not learn by itself. So that's one of the
important considerations. The second thing--
and this is something that we have also always sort of
considered from the very start-- is a focus on root
node problems, problems that are so fundamental
that once you unlock them, they have implications for a
number of different applications downstream. And protein folding, as
I said, had implications for drug discovery
to design new enzymes for plastic
decomposition and so on. And the same thing is
true for materials. If you can understand
material discovery in a transformational
way, that has so many different applications. HANNAH FRY: It's
much more effective than only looking at, say, one
particular drug for Alzheimer's or whatever it might be. So then, do you have a list? Is there a whiteboard
somewhere in this building where you have a list
of potential projects and then you're evaluating
them on that basis? Which ones have got good data? Which ones are more important
problems than others? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Absolutely. We are constantly doing that. And there is a
lot of uncertainty that we have to deal with. So we approach the problem
in a scientific manner. We conduct experiments. We do exploration studies
and see, oh, yes, we are making progress. Some of our assumptions
were correct. So that means that we can
now make that big conviction commitment to that problem. And the other difference in
how we are operating is-- most of the topics that
I've mentioned to you-- we have very focused teams
that pursue these problems. And they are not
operating at the level of six months or 12 months. They are operating at
the level of many years. They are dedicating
researchers and engineers in those teams, are
dedicating their whole careers to that topic. So we take that
responsibility very seriously, as to which are the
right problems that we should be working on. Like if we believe
that a problem can be done at Oxford or MIT or
Harvard or Berkeley or Imperial, we wouldn't want to
work on it, because we want to work on the
problems that really require a scale and the type of
multidisciplinary team that we have to come
together to solve them. HANNAH FRY: Well, let's
pick another topic then, because I think the big thing
that the world's got very excited about in the
last couple of years is generative AI and in
particular large language models. Have you started to incorporate
large language models into your research for science? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. So we are looking at
it across the board. I mean, there are two main
themes that we are exploring. One is that, till now, most
of the work that we have been doing in the
scientific areas, we were using structured data. So this is data, whether
it's genomic data or protein structure prediction
data, where you have sequences and structures-- HANNAH FRY: They're directly
connected to each other. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. The tables, right? But there's a lot of scientific
intuition and knowledge which is embedded in scientific
publications in free form text. And how do you learn
from that experience, from the diary entries
of key scientists that have worked in that area
over the last many centuries? So large language models
give us the ability to now ingest all that data
and to learn from all of it. So that's one key idea. The other way is
basically where you use the large language
model to generate answers in certain domains. And one example of
that is our project on algorithmic discovery,
aptly named FunSearch, which stands for function search. HANNAH FRY: Is much
better named FunSearch. I'm much prefer that as well. PUSHMEET KOHLI: I think our
team was very proud of-- HANNAH FRY: Of that. PUSHMEET KOHLI: --of the name. HANNAH FRY: The fun team. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah, exactly. The fun team working
on FunSearch. They are trying to
discover new algorithms for important problems
in computer science. And the model is asked, here is
a problem, an important problem in computer science,
whether it's a conceptual problem like
the bin packing problem, given a set of boxes
and some items, how do you compactly pack
those items in those boxes? And you might think,
well, I do it at home. What's the relevance of it? That conceptual
problem is everywhere. It's in how delivery companies
go and deliver your groceries to your homes or how
cloud providers schedule computational jobs on different
computers that they have. So it's a very, very important
problem in the real world. And so what this
model does, it then tries to make new--
propose new algorithms. And many of those algorithms
are maybe well known algorithms that it had seen before. And we tell it,
well, that's fine, but try to improve
this particular part and try to refine it. And it goes on
trying to refine it. And sometimes, it
makes a mistake, and we tell it, oh, here's
the mistake that you've made. And it keeps on doing that. We try to get the right
solutions and feed it back. And in that process,
sometimes, it discovers something completely
new that was not known before and ends up improving
the performance or coming up with a new
heuristic or a new algorithm, which solves the problem in a
remarkably different and much more efficient manner. HANNAH FRY: So the reason
it's able to do that, then, is because there's this
deeper network of connections between knowledge that isn't
necessarily visible to us. So is that why
sometimes large language models will hallucinate in a
way that sort of makes sense? So I don't know. Maybe one would say Marie
Curie invented penicillin. And of course, she didn't,
but she did come up with a discovery that was really
important around the same time. So it can swap over
pieces of information, as it were, because it's
seeing deeper connections that aren't visible to us. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Absolutely. It is basically, because it's
operating in that latent space, it feels that there are
certain things which are related to each other. And in the case of the
Marie Curie example, that was a mistake, and
that's a problematic mistake if somebody did
not know about it. It's incorrect information,
but in our case, that's a fine mistake to
have, because what we have is the evaluation
function, which is coupled with
FunSearch, which is coupled with the large language
model, which can call bullshit. HANNAH FRY: Like
a truth detector. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah, exactly. Which can call bullshit and say,
oh, this doesn't make sense, very, very quickly. So then, what matters
is creativity. So if it comes up with
something completely creative, we pick that apart and
say, oh, yeah, great. And the bad stuff
that it says, we are able to filter
it out very easily. HANNAH FRY: But every now and
then, the creative stuff it says is revealing something
more about the underlying network of knowledge. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: It's basically like
you've harnessed hallucinations for good. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. Hallucinations are good
in this particular case if you can somehow
leverage creativity and keep the good part and
filter out all the invalid part. HANNAH FRY: So don't let
the fun team know that. They can call themselves the
hallucinations for good team. No, might get ahead
of themselves. So is it finding stuff that
wasn't known to computer scientists and mathematicians? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes, absolutely. So in fact, with
this method, we were able to get a new result
in computer science. So there is a very interesting
problem called the cap set problem, which is trying
to find independent sets in a structured graph. It's like a computational
problem where you have a graph and you are trying to find
certain sort of nodes and edges, which certain properties. And it has been studied
for a long time. It's a very interesting
problem in computer science. And what FunSearch was able to
do is was, for the first time, produce a result which nobody
had been able to produce. And not only was it able
to produce that result, but the algorithm, the
program that it had generated to find that result
had extremely interesting substructure
and intuitions that, when mathematicians, who
were working with us saw it, they felt that the
program had extracted a new symmetry in the problem. HANNAH FRY: Oh. So in this hallucination,
it had really stumbled upon something
that had been unexplored but turned out to be true? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. And it was leveraging some
very interesting property about the problem that
we had not told it about. HANNAH FRY: Wow. I can see how that
might be useful. Tell me about the Olympiad. PUSHMEET KOHLI: So there is the
International Maths Olympiad, which is a competition
that students take part in. Some of the best
students around the world come to this competition. It has very hard math problems. And if you perform
well, then you can get a bronze,
silver, or gold medal. The level of problems that are
asked at the International Maths Olympiad are extremely hard. HANNAH FRY: Yes, they are. PUSHMEET KOHLI: The current
generation of AI systems, for example, even, they are not
able to tackle those problems, but-- HANNAH FRY: They require
an incredible level of lateral thinking, of
logic, of deep understanding of mathematical concepts,
despite the fact that they're aimed at schoolchildren. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Absolutely. They're not your normal-- HANNAH FRY: They are
not your normal kids. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes, exactly. HANNAH FRY: They're not
the Demis's of the world. You know what I mean? PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. Exactly. I'm sure. I don't know whether Demis
has participated in one but-- HANNAH FRY: I bet he did. PUSHMEET KOHLI: --but
they are exceptional. And it has been a
long-standing challenge for a computational
system to be able to solve any of these problems,
because these are extremely hard mathematics problems. And mathematics, unlike
the game of chess or Go, is an open-ended
environment in the sense that it does not have a
specific number of moves. The moves are infinite. HANNAH FRY: And
in any direction. PUSHMEET KOHLI: And
in any direction. So it's an extremely large space
that you are reasoning over. And so the more
sophisticated AI systems are not able to tackle any
of these sort of problems. So we had a system
called AlphaGeometry, which, for the first time,
showed that an AI system can solve geometry problems that
are at the International Math Olympiad level. HANNAH FRY: So this is like-- I mean, how can
we describe them? So you get a picture of
some circles and triangles and squares and
things, and then you'll be asked something about-- I don't know-- how to calculate
some seemingly impossible thing from this image
based only on what you understand about the
rules of circles and squares and triangles. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes,
something like that. HANNAH FRY: It's really hard. PUSHMEET KOHLI: It's quite hard. And you really have to
understand not only geometry very well, but you also need
to be able to plan ahead and think about what kind
of solution can make sense, can lead you to
the final answer. HANNAH FRY: So how does
AlphaGeometry work, then? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So
AlphaGeometry, how it works, it transforms this problem,
which is given sometimes in text, sometimes in image,
into a formal language, into its own domain-specific
language in which it can reason about the problem,
and then it tries to solve the problem
in that language. Now, what the AlphaGeometry
did was very smart. They generated a very, very
large number of problems, synthetically
generated problems, in that language and the
corresponding solutions. So with this, they could train
the machine learning model on the hundreds of
thousands of these problems. And then that made it
extremely effective in, given a new problem of
that form, it could solve it. HANNAH FRY: It just knows
from its own experience. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yes. HANNAH FRY: Amazing. And actually, I can see
the similarities there, again, between that and
the material science thing is like, OK, let's
just use combinatorics. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: It's just
like, roll the dice, come up with loads of random
things in the beginning, and then narrow it down and
narrow it down, narrow it down until eventually it can look
at a single problem and say, I know how to solve it. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: I'm quite
jealous of your job. We have gone on a wild
list of topics here, but what are you
hoping to tackle next? PUSHMEET KOHLI: So I think
there are many problems that remain unsolved. In any area that
we're working on, whether it's
understanding proteins, whether it's understanding
the genome, whether it's understanding the weather,
whether it's understanding a material science, there
is just so much work that still remains to be done. We were talking about materials. We are only making
predictions about stability of these materials,
but how do you extend that to making the
prediction about properties of these materials and
then synthesizing them? HANNAH FRY: Have you
got a favorite, though? Is there one that you're like,
that's the one I really want? PUSHMEET KOHLI: No, you
can't ask me that question. HANNAH FRY: I can. I did. PUSHMEET KOHLI: No. I think all-- HANNAH FRY: Not all
your babies are equal. You got to pick a favorite. PUSHMEET KOHLI: I'm
a computer scientist, I love the computer
science work, but-- all of them have different
elements which are on the day, they are like, wow,
this is so amazing. When you are trying to learn a
policy that is going to control the magnets of a fusion reactor,
the day that that experiment happens, regardless of
what's happening, I'm-- HANNAH FRY: You care
most about that. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah, exactly. HANNAH FRY: Is there
one that you think, though, will have
the biggest impact? PUSHMEET KOHLI: I
think the work that we are doing in
understanding biology and in understanding chemistry
and materials, I think, it's so fundamental. HANNAH FRY: Such
root node problems. PUSHMEET KOHLI: Yeah. There are such
root node problems that it's difficult to even
limit what the impact would be. The effects are
difficult to predict. HANNAH FRY: Absolutely. I'm very much looking forward to
coming back and talking to you again to see what other massive
things have been happening and push me. Thank you very much
for joining me. PUSHMEET KOHLI:
Thank you, Hannah. HANNAH FRY: What I'm struck by
after meeting Pushmeet is that, the thing about science, it
moves really slowly, and then, every now and then, maybe
once in a generation, you get this seismic shift. You get a big step
forward that fundamentally changes our understanding. But the thing about this shift
of using artificial intelligence for science is that
this isn't just going to make a difference
to physics or one equation in cosmology. It's that this is
going to transform all of science and in
the most fundamental way. And OK, I know that I
sound like I've just swallowed the hype
here, but don't just take my word for this. Go and ask your friendly
neighborhood scientist what they think of
what is happening here. Because the people
who really understand what this means for the
world behind the scenes, they don't have words
for how big this shift is or is going to be. You have been listening to
"Google DeepMind, The Podcast" with me, Professor Hannah Fry. If you enjoyed that episode,
do subscribe to our YouTube channel, and you
can also find us on your podcast
platform of choice. Now, we have lots
more to explore on this podcast,
including a deep dive into how AI could
enhance education. Plus, should AI assistants

"Google DeepMind, The Podcast." [MUSIC PLAYING]
