=== VIDEO: 64pndvbbokA (https://youtu.be/64pndvbbokA) ===


HANNAH FRY: Welcome back
to "Google DeepMind-- The Podcast," with me, your
host, Professor Hannah Fry. [MUSIC PLAYING] I think it's fair to say that
generative AI, as we have it now, is a bit of a mismatch for
our science fiction fantasies about what technology
might become. But it is worth remembering that
this isn't the only possibility. There are other
forms of AI forms, forms which are by their very
design, autonomous entities. These AI are firmly in
the realm of robot butlers or a virtual concierge that
anticipates your every whim before you even have it. And OK, we might have to wait
a little while for those two particular examples. But I'm talking here about
artificial intelligence with agency, AI that has
built-in wants and objectives and the ability to independently
make decision after decision in pursuit of its own goals. This kind of AI are
known as agents, and while agents
haven't yet exploded into the public
consciousness quite as much as generative AI
has, trust us, they are going to be a very big deal. And in the meantime, a very
good training ground for agents is the world of video games. Think about it--
perfectly packaged, neatly constrained environments
where the agents can run wild, work out the rules
for themselves, and learn how to
handle autonomy. If you've been following
us since the beginning, then you will know
that AI in games is something that DeepMind
has a proud heritage in. And so to explain
today, we are joined by Frederic Besse, who is
a Senior Staff Research Engineer at Google DeepMind. Frederic has a PhD
in Computer Vision and was one of the
earliest people to join the DeepMind
team back in 2015, when there were only about
100 researchers here. And before the decade he
spent at the cutting-edge of artificial
intelligence, he also spent some time working
in the visual effects industry on how to integrate
CGI into real footage. Frederic, thank you so
much for joining us. With a CV like that, I can
see why they wanted you. FREDERIC BESSE: Well, thanks. HANNAH FRY: OK, well,
so I think it's probably best if we start off
with some definitions. FREDERIC BESSE: Sure. HANNAH FRY: So, I mean,
I've talked briefly about what agents are there, but
how do you define them? FREDERIC BESSE: So,
in my mind, an agent is a very general concept. And I define it as an entity
that can act in an environment. I think action is
what defines an agent. And an environment,
on the other side, is a place which can provide
observations for the agent and which the agent
can interact with. And so what's
important to understand is that the agent, by
acting into the environment, will change the
environment, which is crucial for developing
AI systems that can do useful things
and understand the consequences
of their actions in environments, in
games, in the world, on the internet, et cetera. HANNAH FRY: This sounds
like something that exists beyond just AI, though. I mean, autopilot on planes,
would that count as an agent? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, absolutely. So maybe we can give a
few examples of agents because it's a very
general concept. So humans are agents
because we can act. We observe the world with
our eyes and our ears. We have proprioception. We know where our limbs are. So we are agents. We can affect the world. Similarly, robots as well. But if you go back
to maybe decades ago where we had agents such as
Autopilot, which we handcrafted the logic of those
agents, we were programming them to do
what we wanted them to do. So they were different
from the modern agents that we can talk about later
on, but these were indeed examples of agents. HANNAH FRY: So, all right. If that's an agent then,
is that the same thing as autonomy or having agency? Are these all slightly,
subtly different? FREDERIC BESSE: So I think
they are subtly different. I think an agent can
act and do things that it has been
programmed to do, and that's agency, being
able to take actions. Now autonomy is more the
skill or the property of acting by itself to
accomplish a certain task. And there are various
levels of autonomy. So if we have a self-driving
car, which will take decision to best navigate
the roads, you could say that there is a
certain level of autonomy. Some agents are
much less autonomous and will require a lot
more of your input. And then on the other
end of the spectrum, you could have an agent
that goes in the world and learn new things,
explore, which we don't really have to instruct much. So there are a spectrum
in the space of autonomy. HANNAH FRY: Is
part of that almost a hierarchy of the type of
objectives that you're setting? Like, I don't know,
the autonomous action of picking up a cup
and putting it down is slightly different to
deciding what's for lunch. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, because
we are training agents to do simple things because
this is a starting point. But having an agent that can
cook for you your whole lunch-- HANNAH FRY: And decide what
you want as well would be nice. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, as well. It would be a recommender system
will require a lot of sub steps and sub tasks too, and
solve those tasks properly as well first. So we call those
high-level tasks compared to short-horizon
tasks, which will be very basic, almost mechanical actions,
like grab an object, put the objects on the table,
compared to cook lunch. HANNAH FRY: Yeah,
like the robots you see in factories, for
example, versus the type of ones that we have in science fiction. But is that the aim? Are we talking about
robots eventually that will be in our homes? FREDERIC BESSE: I think so. So what we are trying to do
is to develop general agents. And we want to
reach a point, which we call AGI, Artificial
General Intelligence, which is a point where we will have
agents and AI systems that will be truly general, as
general as humans, will be able to adapt to new
situations, reason like we do, and even surpass our
capabilities in doing tasks. Once we reach this level of
competency in AI systems, this will unlock
a lot of potential for what we can do as humans. Those AI systems will
be able to accelerate our scientific
progress, for example, or do a lot of tasks which
maybe we are not very good for. For example, maybe driving,
or help you in your home, help you do shopping online. I think there are some
applications which we can't even begin to conceive
because this is such a technological leap that I
think we will just discover it as we go. But we do believe that building
such general agents, which can act into an environment,
is key to achieve AGI. HANNAH FRY: That's
interesting in the way that you're describing it,
that some of these agents are going to be
virtual, essentially, and then some of them will
be actually physically embedded in an environment. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So I think it all goes back to
what's your observation space and what's your action space. So we can have physical agents,
embodied physical agents, like robots, which will be able
to do things in the real world, which will be very useful. And we already, as you-- HANNAH FRY: My robot
butler, thank you. FREDERIC BESSE: Exactly. But also virtual agents, which
work not in the real world, but in an abstract
virtual space. This could be still a physical
space, for example, games, which we use in our research to
also demonstrate and understand what those agents can do. But also, you could
have an agent that doesn't have any physical,
even virtual action space, but instead it
can make programs. For example, write Python
code, build software for you, which is not a physical
space, but it's still an agent because it can take actions,
it can compile the function, it can look on the internet. And so, this is
still very valuable. HANNAH FRY: How is this
different to the chatbots that we've seen from
large language models? Because they can do a lot
of that stuff too, right? They can search
the internet, they can understand your instructions
and make decisions, sort of? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So chatbots, what you get
out of chatbots generally is a chat output. So they produce
natural language. Now, there are some
capabilities in some chatbots which could go and search
the internet for you. But this space is still
fairly constrained to just being either natural
language or specific functions that the programmer adds to
the chatbot to do things. Now those chatbots, when
they speak with you, I mean, in a way, you are the
environment of the chatbot, right? What the chatbot
says will affect you and will affect what you
ask the chatbot next. But maybe there is not
as much consequence as if the chatbot
or the agent was acting in an environment
which drastically changes when the agent takes some action. So it's a bit more of a
passive type of agent. We could call it
an agent, but it's very different from an agent
that acts in the real world. And the way also those
models are trained, they are trained with
a lot of data that are scraped from the internet. But they are not trained
in an environment to try to do things, fail,
understand why they failed, and try again. So this is a
difference between some of the way those models are
trained with human data. HANNAH FRY: So then, should we
be thinking of these as separate or can you combine the two? Can you combine large
language models and an agent that has an objective? FREDERIC BESSE: So
you can actually, there are some robotics
paper from Google DeepMind that is called RT-2 and
RT-X, which are doing that. So they are training. So a language model
has a lot of knowledge from the internet scale data
that it has been trained on. So if you want, it has
a lot of common sense and it understands
a bunch of concepts, but it doesn't have the ability
to actuate joints of a robot. But you can text
this model and train it to produce joint activation
for your robotic arm, for example. That's what they've
done in that paper. HANNAH FRY: I guess almost
the language model gives the algorithm some sort
of inverted commas, conceptual
understanding of things. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, I
think that's what is great about those big models is
that they have such knowledge and understand
concepts and words and what you chat to them
about that they can make plans. But all of this is
in language space. And we need to bridge the gap
towards acting in the real world or in the virtual world. HANNAH FRY: To go off
and actually enact it? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. HANNAH FRY: What is it
about games, though? Why is it useful to
start with games? FREDERIC BESSE: So I think games
offer a very rich experience and games are very varied. So if you look at
how many games there are on computers, for example,
you have tens of thousands of games. And each game offers
you different scenarios, different environments,
different things you could be doing in the game. So we think it's a
great proving ground to train AI systems to
match human performance. There are other
advantages in games. So they are virtual, so
they are easy to scale. You could instantiate a lot
of different games in parallel compared to physical
systems, which are constraints to the real world. They are also safe. So if things go wrong in games,
it doesn't really matter. It's just a game. Games are a place
where humans can interact with agents, for
example, in multiplayer games. And games are fun as
well for humans to play. And I think it's a great
source of experience data that we can collect. HANNAH FRY: Because you've
already got agents already in non-player characters, NPCs
floating around the place. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, yes. So I think that's a
very interesting point. Historically, and actually
it's still the case, when game developers create
agents inside of games, it's a very different beast to
the type of agents that we make. Those agents have access to
the internal code of the game. And the game developers
just write the logic, which works great, and they
are fast and they can do pretty impressive things. HANNAH FRY: Things
like stand around, and then if someone
approaches you, attack them. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah, there is
some pretty complicated logic you could put in the game,
in the agents in the game, in the NPCs. But there is a limit in how
complicated you can describe a behavior in terms of code. And that's where deep learning
and modern agents come in, because those are not-- the
logic inside those agents is not handcrafted by humans. Instead, we subject the agent
to a lot of experience data, and the agent will learn
the behavior for itself in a softer way
with more nuances. And it can pick up on very
complex behavior, which maybe wouldn't be feasible
to write down in code, in programming language. HANNAH FRY: Rather than
just following rules, it's sort of working out how to
achieve its objective itself? FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: DeepMind's
got quite a long history of playing around with games. Maybe that a bit
trivializes it slightly considering the things that
they've achieved by doing so. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So back in 2015, there
was the first breakthrough in neural network agents,
called DQN, for Deep Q Network, in which the agent was
trained to play Atari. And the breakthrough here
is that the agent was only using pixels as the
observation space. HANNAH FRY: So Atari is
like "Space Invaders," break out the one with the
bricks where you've got a paddle and a ball bouncing around. Like, the games you
had in the 1980s. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah, so
the arcade games, exactly. HANNAH FRY: And the only thing
it would take as an input were the pixels on the screen? FREDERIC BESSE: So the
pixels of the screen. And there was also the
concept of reward, which is like how many points you get. And that is very important
for those agents to learn. Because what those agents are
doing and how they are trained, they are trained using a method
called reinforcement learning, which aims at maximizing this
score, maximizing the reward. And so combining deep learning
with deep neural network and reinforcement learning
yielded the DQN agent, which was first of its kind. And that was a very big
milestone for DeepMind. Then another important
agent that was built was AlphaGo, which
was an agent that tries to learn to play the game
of Go, which is a board game. HANNAH FRY:
Notoriously difficult. FREDERIC BESSE:
Notoriously difficult. I think it was uncertain
whether AI could ever beat the game of Go. But then that was
proven to be wrong. HANNAH FRY: When
AlphaGo beat Lee Sedol. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, exactly. So that was a huge result.
Then if you continue along the history, so this
is a non-exhaustive list because there is a
lot of research that has been happening
also in between all these important milestones. There was AlphaZero, which is
taking the agents that play Go, and also it could play chess
and Shogi, to the next level. And this agent was trained
without any human data. So while AlphaGo
was trained using a mix of human data and
self-play and reinforcement learning, AlphaZero was
trained without any human data. And just starting from
scratch with self-play, it discovered the
best chess moves, and that was very
interesting to see. HANNAH FRY: Extraordinary,
that taking the human input out made it better. FREDERIC BESSE: Well, yes. So it's interesting
because you can actually look at what are the most
optimal moves for chess, which is a fascinating find. HANNAH FRY: But then it
moved into computer games again, didn't it? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So I think one next
very important paper was the AlphaStar paper, which is an
agent that is playing "StarCraft II," which is a game,
competitive game. That's very
different environment from, say, Go or Chess. So one of the challenge here
is that in "StarCraft II," you only see partial information
from your environment. There is this thing called fog
of war where you cannot see what your enemy is doing. And so you really have
to take this into account during your strategy,
while in Go, in chess, you see the whole board
in front of you. So you have access to the
whole state of the game. Also, the agent has to play in
real time because "StarCraft II," once you play against
a human at test time, you really have to play
as fast as you can, so that you cannot
take minutes to think. And the "StarCraft II"
agent was able to beat very, very good humans at the game. HANNAH FRY: So now this is
one step on even further. So it's like at every
step, you remove something that made it easy. So Atari, you get
points for every action. chess, you don't. In chess, you get to
see the entire board. In "StarCraft," you don't. In "StarCraft,"
it's competitive, in what you're
doing now, it isn't. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So those agents were trying
to maximize the score and to win the game, while what
we do in SIMA is very different. We train agents to
follow instructions. HANNAH FRY: What's
this project called? FREDERIC BESSE: Our
project is called SIMA. That stands for Scalable
Instructable Multi-world Agent. And we used various different
video games and also research environments to train an
agent to follow instructions that we ask it to do. Those instructions are fairly
simple that you can typically carry out in 5 to 10 seconds,
such as pick up the apple, or turn around, climb up
the ladder, et cetera. And so there is no score. And we use the games
really as containers, as walls in which
we can carry out a whole bunch of instructions. And the amount of instruction,
the number of instructions that you can come up with is
very large due to the fact that we use natural language to
formulate those instructions. So this poses other challenges. We really want to make
our agents to be general. So while a Go agent only
learns to beat the game of Go, we are interested in making
an agent that can really understand what you're saying,
understand the context, understand the environment, and
then carry out this instruction. And so we are aiming
for generality. And I think generality is
a key property that we want in our agents to pursue AGI. HANNAH FRY: What do you
mean by sandbox games? FREDERIC BESSE:
So a sandbox game is a game where the
player can basically do whatever they want within the
space of what the game offers. So it's a game which
has natural landscape. You can craft, you can build,
you can cook, you can explore. HANNAH FRY: Like "Minecraft." FREDERIC BESSE:
Like "Minecraft." That's what we call a sandbox
game because there is not really a goal to the game. Some games are sandbox with a
goal, so it's a bit of a mix. But yeah, you just have fun
and do a bunch of things. HANNAH FRY: Like in
an actual sandbox. FREDERIC BESSE: Exactly, yes. HANNAH FRY: Well, how about you? Are you a gamer? FREDERIC BESSE:
Yes, very much so. I was given a Gameboy
for my sixth birthday. HANNAH FRY: Sixth birthday? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. HANNAH FRY: Lucky thing. [LAUGHTER] FREDERIC BESSE:
Yeah, it was amazing. I think my first
game was "Tetris." And yeah, I was amazed
by being able to play those games in the car. Car trips were never the same. HANNAH FRY: Yeah, that's true. And as an adult, did
you go beyond "Tetris?" FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, of course. I remember when we
at home got DSL, which could give you basically
constant internet access. I was very happy
because I could finally play online games
without being worried about the counter, how much
time do I spend on the internet. So yeah, online games,
competitive games, sandbox games, and [INAUDIBLE]
games, all of this I do quite like. HANNAH FRY: I
mean, so you've got basically the perfect experience
then to be in this space. FREDERIC BESSE: Looks like it. HANNAH FRY: The new
projects that you're working on now,
these sandbox games, I mean, they are
quite a big departure. How hard is it to get an
agent to achieve things in an environment
where you don't have that really clearly
defined metric of success? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, so it is a
very different style of building an agent training from before. There is some prior work,
which we were very inspired by, which was the interactive
agents project, there is a blog post on the
DeepMind blog post, where they use the
Playhouse environment with a bunch of
objects, and the goal was to get the agent to carry
out instructions in that house. HANNAH FRY: It's like a
simulated environment? FREDERIC BESSE: It's a
simulated environment, yes. It's like a children's
house with colorful objects. And the goal was to take the
teapots and put it on the beds, and there was no reward as well. So we wanted to take
this to the next level. And we also wanted to use
games that are not games that we make ourselves, that are
available for everyone to play. HANNAH FRY: So
basically it's the games that you guys are playing? Is that what it is? FREDERIC BESSE: Some
of them are, indeed. [LAUGHTER] HANNAH FRY: I just want more
time to spend on this game that I've been
doing, so I'm going to build an agent to do it. That's essentially
what it sounds like. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah,
you could do it. And so, yeah, we
got into partnership with game developers
and game studios to be able to use their
games in our research. And we picked games which
are of a certain genre. So we wanted 3D games because
we were interested in these embodied 3D setup. We wanted to be able to define
our own objective with language. So if you go into
"Valheim," for example, which is a Viking
survival simulation, you can make up a lot
of tasks for the agents. You can say, go get
some mud, build a house, craft jackets, et cetera. HANNAH FRY: Then how on
Earth do you train an agent when you don't have rewards? FREDERIC BESSE: So what we
did is that we collected data from humans playing the game. And then we used a technique
called imitation learning, which teaches the agent to
just mimic and predict what a human would do next. So given all of the observation,
given what you've done before, what would the human do? And that's how we train
the behavior of the agent. So there is no reward. We just imitate
humans, which-- yeah. HANNAH FRY: Is it
probabilistic, then? Is it sort of like, in
this sort of situation, humans acted in
this way, so we'll act in that way with
this probability? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, exactly. So all the actions of the
agent are probabilistic. So before the agent acts, there
is a probability distribution, which needs to sample
and pick an action from. Often it's the
most likely action, but sometimes you can take
an unlikely action, which can lead to unforeseen behavior. Sometimes it's a good behavior. Sometimes it's not. HANNAH FRY: Go on,
give you an example. FREDERIC BESSE:
Well, so for example, we have a game such
as "Goat Simulator." HANNAH FRY: Tell us the
objective of "Goat Simulator." FREDERIC BESSE: So the
objective of "Goat Simulator" is to basically cause mayhem
as a goat in the world. And you can drive cars,
you can jump on objects. HANNAH FRY: As a goat. FREDERIC BESSE: As a goat. Yeah, you're a goat. You can have wings and
you can fly as well. But it's a perfect sandbox
game because you can do so many different things. And so in "Goat Simulator,"
there is a key which causes the goat to become all floppy
and ragdoll on the floor. I'm not too sure what's the-- when you would
actually as a player use this, maybe to pretend. HANNAH FRY: Play dead. FREDERIC BESSE: Exactly. But sometimes when we
play test the agents, it ragdolls on the
floor, which brings some funny moments in the
team when we do the playtest. HANNAH FRY: Just expecting it
to barge into a crowd of people and it just ragdolls. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. Yes. That can happen. HANNAH FRY: Can I see
what it looks like? FREDERIC BESSE:
Yeah, I'll show you. So here, let's start
by "Goat Simulator 3." HANNAH FRY: My fave. FREDERIC BESSE: So we
have a video of the agent here, which the instruction
is to go to a green object. [LAUGHTER] HANNAH FRY: We've got a goat. And we've got quite
realistic environment. And we've got the goat
trotting forwards. There's plenty of green, which
the goat is walking past, but it's doing a big
jump into a pond. And in the pond,
there is an inflatable in the shape of a snake, which
the goat has successfully reached. FREDERIC BESSE: It's green. HANNAH FRY: It's
definitely green. And it is an object. FREDERIC BESSE:
It's an object, yes. HANNAH FRY: And it did a
jump there to get there. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah. So sometimes the agent picks
up on some human peculiarities. For example, humans,
when they play games, are often bored to
take the longest path. For example, climbing
up the stairs. If you have a
jetpack, you're just going to jetpack
over the stairs. So one of the first
agents where we asked, get up to the platform,
instead of taking the stairs the
long way, the agent just jetpacked over the stairs. So it does learn
about human behavior. HANNAH FRY: The
strange shortcuts. I also noticed that rather than
just walking into the pond, it jumped, which is exactly
what I would do as a human. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: You want
to make a splash. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. HANNAH FRY: OK. All right. Well, I mean, it succeeded. It succeeded. FREDERIC BESSE: So this
is the game "Valheim," which is a Viking
survival sandbox game. And the task here that we
asked the agent to carry out is to pick up mushrooms. HANNAH FRY: OK, so again,
another very beautifully crafted environment. The camera is
moving around, which I guess is the agent doing
that, moving the camera around. It has found quite quickly
some mushrooms, shot them out of the ground-- I don't know whether the Vikings
had that particular ability-- and just carrying on
wandering through the forest. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah. HANNAH FRY: OK. So definitely like camera
control and movement in that 3D environment, it's mastered. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So I think because all the
games that we use are 3D games, the camera control is very
similar from all those games. So I there is a
lot of experience to learn from in terms of
controlling the camera. You can see that the movement
of the camera is human-like. So it hesitates. It's not very robotic, just aim
on the mushroom and go forward. And that's because it's
trained from human data. If you train an agent
with reinforcement learning to do the same
task, you could detect-- HANNAH FRY: As opposed
to imitation learning? FREDERIC BESSE: As opposed
to imitation learning, the agent would have very linear
and robotic camera motion. It would just go
to the mushroom. It needs to optimize its reward. So it will not spend
any time hesitating. HANNAH FRY: When you're
instructing the agent, you're not explaining
what a mushroom is or what a mushroom might
look like in the game? FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah, so it gets
that from the training data. So in this training
data, there is a bunch of sequences of humans
interacting with mushrooms, picking up mushrooms. And so it just
learns from the data. So an interesting task we
could ask the agent here is, if you hold out,
if the agent has never seen mushrooms or maybe
never seen red mushrooms, but maybe it has
seen blue mushrooms, and you ask the agent to
pick up the red mushrooms, is the agent able to generalize
color and object type? So those type of questions
are the questions we are interested in. HANNAH FRY: Which is
definitely demonstrating a sort of conceptual
understanding of the environment that it's in. FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah, it would. Yes. We're looking for this
generality and transfer between concepts and trying
to put things together, new concepts together
to carry out a task. And yeah, this will
show understanding of what a mushroom is and
what the color red is. HANNAH FRY: In a way, the way
that you describe the imitation part of it, the
imitation learning, it sounds a little
bit like you're doing a sort of computer game
version of predictive text, almost. Like, what has been done
in this environment before, how do you repeat it? FREDERIC BESSE: It
is exactly that. HANNAH FRY: Is it? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, so when
you train language models on language, the objective
in which you train, at least the initial
model, is to predict what's the next token. I mean, if it's assuming that
it's all human-generated text, what would the human say next? What word would
the human say next? Which is very similar
to this, which is, what would the human do next in
terms of the keyboard and mouse action? So you're right, it is pretty
much the same technique. HANNAH FRY: The same ideas. But the key difference here
is that you're instructing it. You're saying, this is
the objective for you and go and do that in a
way that a human would do? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So it's given this instruction,
what would the human do next? But the language
models are also, in a way, prompted
with some prior text. So they would say,
given that this text is in your prompt, what is the
next most probable word that you should output? So it's not very different. The maybe infrastructure and
architecture of the agent itself is a bit
different, because we here need to produce a very different
modality, which is action in keyboard and mouse space. HANNAH FRY: It
sounds like it's kind of exploring the
network of actions that people have
taken in the past. Does it do original
things, then? FREDERIC BESSE: So it
will not do anything that humans haven't
done, per se, but the agent is
able to generalize to unseen environments, so in
a way that's kind of novel. So it's like, you're
a tennis player. And suddenly, you're asked
to do a badminton match, right, as a human. Because you've
learned tennis, you have some reflexes and
mechanics that you know. You know how to hold the
racket, how to strike a ball. And so you're going to do
fairly well at badminton. Not the best, but you're not
going to be beginner level. And in our agent,
we see the same. So what we've done is
that we trained the agent on all environments
except one, and then we test the agent on
that one environment. And we can see that
the agent is able to do a few things at a reasonable
level in that environment. So in a way, I don't know if
you can say those are novel, but it definitely shows
some promise with respect to generalization and
generalizing to unseen environments. HANNAH FRY: So, OK, tell me how
well does it perform overall, then? FREDERIC BESSE:
Maybe I can tell you about two interesting results. HANNAH FRY: Yes, please. FREDERIC BESSE: So
first of all, we observed that when we
train an agent on all of the environments,
it performs better than an agent that
is only trained on each singular
individual environment. HANNAH FRY: So you train
an agent on "No Man's Sky," "Goat Simulator 3," "Valheim,"
and then it does better in, for example,
"Goat Simulator 3" than an agent that is trained
only in "Goat Simulator 3?" FREDERIC BESSE: Exactly, yes. So that was a very
good result for us because it demonstrates
that the experience that you get from training
on variety of game make your skill in each
individual game stronger. So what I like to think of
is, let's say as a human, maybe to go back to the tennis
example, maybe I train on tennis and I train on badminton. And maybe I'll be better
at both of those compared to if I had only
trained on, say, tennis because maybe I gained some
extra skills and subtlety in the type of actions I need to
do from being exposed to a wider range of skills and experience. HANNAH FRY: Is it also partly
they're just exposed to more data, so more of how humans
manipulate 3D environments, more of different kinds of
things that humans might do? FREDERIC BESSE: Yeah. So games, different games
offer different situations. So the agent can extract
knowledge from those situations and maybe relate them to other
situations from other games. And so it just accumulates
and consolidates the behavior into something that is
better than not having seen this other experience, which in
a way, our hypothesis when we started the project was the
same hypothesis as the language model. Training a lot of data will
make some interesting properties emerge, such as being able
to generalize and transfer. And so, yeah, that's what
we also observed here. HANNAH FRY: But
then I think also, that result isn't immediately
obvious because it could well have been the case that actually
all the things that it learns from "No Man's Sky," like,
confuse it when it goes back to "Goat Simulator 3." FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So we were worried about
that because we thought maybe there will be a destructive
interference between games. That's true. We picked those games to all be
3D because we thought there was a chance that some of the
experience would transfer between games. If we had trained on
"Tetris" and "No Man's Sky," we wouldn't expect any
positive interference here. HANNAH FRY: Slightly different
environment, isn't it? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. But because all of those games
have some similar concepts, same way of navigating
around, moving the camera, we thought maybe there would be
some interesting and positive outcome to training
on multiple games. HANNAH FRY: You told
me that there were two, there were two big results. FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So the other game,
the other result, sorry, which I
mentioned before, was that when you train an agent
on all environments but one, it performs roughly as well
on this held-out environment as the agent that has only been
trained on that environment. HANNAH FRY: OK,
wait, wait, wait. So, right, you've
got eight games. You train an agent on them and
leave off "Goat Simulator 3." It's never seen "Goat
Simulator 3" before. And then you compare it against
an agent that's seen nothing else but "Goat Simulator 3." FREDERIC BESSE: Exactly. HANNAH FRY: And then you test
them in "Goat Simulator 3." FREDERIC BESSE: Yes,
and the SIMA agent here performs nearly as well
as the environment. We call it the
environment expert because it's an
expert in goat expert. HANNAH FRY: Goal expert, sure. FREDERIC BESSE:
So, yes, it doesn't perform as well, on average, but
it does perform reasonably well. So it has learned to
generalize to unseen. All the other games have
different avatars, for example. The agent has not-- HANNAH FRY: There's no
goats anywhere else. There's no goats in space. FREDERIC BESSE: But the agent
is able to move the goat around and perform a few tasks. Of course, there are some
actions in "Goat Simulator" which are specific and
unique to "Goat Simulator," and that we don't-- HANNAH FRY: There's no
ragdolling astronauts. FREDERIC BESSE: No, and
we don't expect the agent to be able to get that
because it has never seen it, or it has never seen
experience from it. But other things like
navigation, maybe concepts about objects and
colors, that is something that it can achieve. HANNAH FRY: So how
do you expand that beyond these sort
of tasks that last for 10 seconds, 15 seconds
of jumping and picking up a mushroom? FREDERIC BESSE: We
are working currently on trying to find
ways of doing that. First of all, there is a lot to
improve because our agent is not at human level, even for
those short horizon tasks. For longer horizon tasks, we
are looking into methods which would allow us to do that. You mentioned large
language models before, which are able to maybe reason
on a higher time horizon, longer time horizon. So maybe combining those models
with our agent could be a way, but that's future work. HANNAH FRY: Can I play
alongside one of these things, just to make my experience
of the game better? Can I cheat, basically? FREDERIC BESSE:
So some agents do find ways of exploiting the
games to do things that were not originally intended. So in a way, you could call
that cheating, I guess. But playing, like, once we have
very competent AI systems, which could do all sorts of things,
playing games with you could also be one
of the things you want to do with those systems. That would be quite
fun, I think, especially in these sandbox games. If you had an agent,
which you could say, OK, now I want to play hide-and-seek
in "Goat Simulator," and let's say it's multiplayer,
you have two goats, and you can play
whatever you want. I think it would be
quite fun to interact with an agent in exactly
the way you want. So the way we currently
sometimes use large language models is we prompt them
to do all sorts of things, write a poem, do some
role play, write code. If we had the same
ability, but also with an agent that has an avatar
in game, which you could prompt, I think that could be
lots of fun for gamers. HANNAH FRY: Yeah, it's
like, level, level, level up on an NPC, isn't it? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. HANNAH FRY: OK, so what is
the big objective here, then? FREDERIC BESSE:
So I think what we are interested in
in the SIMA team is to build truly general agents. So we use games as a
platform to drive innovation and breakthrough into
this particular space so that we can get
a step closer to AGI and understand how to build
those truly general agents. So we are really in for
the research towards AGI. HANNAH FRY: What's
the definition of AGI that DeepMind work to? FREDERIC BESSE: I think
the definition of AGI is an agent that is as general
and capable as a human. And I think the
general is key here. It could be better than a human,
but at least at human level. HANNAH FRY: So you can
pick it up and drop it into different
environments and it can do as well as a human can? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes. So, I think if you think
about gaming, as a human, maybe there is a
new game coming out. I install it and very quickly I
will be able to play this game. Maybe I need to go
through the tutorial because there are some
specificity to the game that I need to learn, but
in general, I think we are very good at adapting
to those new situations, to those new environments,
and be competent at it. And we really want the
same for our agents. We want to drop the agent
in a new environment or show it a new game and
observe that they can, indeed, also adapt and become competent
at those new situations. HANNAH FRY: Well, you've sort of
done that already, haven't you? FREDERIC BESSE: There is
still a long way to go. So, our agents have been trained
to only perform simple actions, but really, there is so much
more skills and scenarios which we could use
to train our agents. How do we make an agent that
could handle 1,000 different games? What does it take? There are some breakthroughs
that needs to happen. HANNAH FRY: So then
let's say that you manage that, which I'm sure you will. What happens then? Is the idea that you can take
that learning and then apply it to really generalist agents
that exist in the environment that we all live in? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes,
there are methods. How did we build the agent? I think the
understanding what method we use to achieve this result is
very important, that we can then apply the same method
to other domains, which we might be interested in. Understanding technical details
of how to build those agents, understanding how to collect
the data, which data is useful, algorithms, how do
we train the agent, I think that's very
important as well. So, yeah, any kind of
innovation and progress we can make towards
understanding the how to make progress in
AGI is what we are after. HANNAH FRY: But
these are lessons that can apply elsewhere? FREDERIC BESSE: They could
apply to other domains. Yes. HANNAH FRY: OK. So, give me an example, then. What kind of agents
should I be hoping for? FREDERIC BESSE: I mean, so are
we talking about the far future? HANNAH FRY: Yeah, go on. FREDERIC BESSE: OK, so I think
I could say an example of agents would be self-driving cars
that are safe and reliable. I think those are
agents which take actions that have consequences. And so having very robust and
reliable self-driving car, which can generalize to
unseen circumstances, would be very important. And I think the generality
here is very important. Because when you
drive, you never know what's going to happen. I mean, if we had agents
in the real world, one of the things we would like
would be for agents to carry out instructions for us. For example, would be
a robot in your house where you say, maybe carry these
heavy objects to the table. That's a task which is
defined in language, which would be very useful. Maybe virtual agents, which
can code and write software would be very useful for us. Maybe agents that can help you
in everyday tasks which you do online, for example, shopping. I mean, we spend a lot of
time researching what to buy. If I want a new
pair of headphones, I might spend
hours to understand which one is good for me. But if I could
have an agent that can do this research for me,
that would be very useful, I think. HANNAH FRY: I'm
just thinking about, I'm just going back to your
AGI stuff, actually, if I may, just to finish on. Do you think that the sparks
of AGI will happen in your lab? FREDERIC BESSE: Well, it's
very difficult to say, but we do hope, yes, that
the methods we develop will help to make
progress towards AGI. I think if we had an agent
that could play any games and carry out any
instruction in any game-- HANNAH FRY: And perform
a superhuman ability? FREDERIC BESSE: Yes, I think
that would be a good milestone to achieve because that would
show human-level intelligence, at least in games. But games are very
rich and sometimes are a bit like the real world. You need to understand
your surroundings. You need to understand objects. You need to understand the
consequence of your actions. Especially if you add
language into the mix because if you add language into
the mix for tasks, then really you could ask the
agent to do anything. So if such agent is
able to carry out any instruction in
any game, I think it would be one
step closer to AGI. HANNAH FRY: Fred, thank you
very much for joining me. FREDERIC BESSE: Thank you
so much for having me. HANNAH FRY: There have
been a few moments now while making this podcast
where we have been here at the early stages of a
project, before the results were grand or headline grabbing,
when there was only the tiniest little spark of
something to see. Like, remember when we
first visited the robot lab to watch them flailing around
in circles, or the first time that we saw AlphaFold when
it was only just starting to unravel the
mysteries of proteins? Well, I think it feels
a lot like this is the stage we're at with SIMA. Because sure,
picking up a mushroom isn't quite the vision
of the AI future that we've been waiting
for, but it's the direction that those agents
are moving in that really holds the secret promise
to what might be possible. Because if we want AI that
can make its own decisions, achieve its own objectives,
operate independently of our instructions, then this
is a vital step along the way. So today, it might
be a mushroom. The question is, what will
it be next time we come back? You've been listening
to "Google DeepMind-- The Podcast," with me,
Professor Hannah Fry. If you like what you just
heard, hit Subscribe on YouTube or follow us on your
favorite podcast platform. We've got an exciting line
up of conversations to come, including an interview
with Pushmeet Kohli, who oversees Science Research
at Google DeepMind. If you have someone
in mind that you'd love to hear from on this
podcast, do leave us a comment. See you next time. [MUSIC PLAYING]
